name: HMS Triumph (i) (N 18)
events: The history of HMS Triumph as compiled on this page is extracted from patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine and was first published here in March 2012.
Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
Also thanks to Mr. Gav Don from the U.K. for his input.
This page was last updated in April 2026.

25 Aug 1939 Around 0001 A/25, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) departed from Dundee to take up a patrol position off the South-West coast of Norway. When the war started this became her 1st war patrol.
For the daily positions of HMS Triumph during this patrol see the map below.

3 Sep 1939 At 2315 A/3, while patrolling between Egersund and Obrestad, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted a darkened small craft bearing 290° [range is unreadable in the logbook]. Course of this vessel was 180°, speed 15 knots. This was considered to be a Norwegian torpedo boat on patrol. HMS Triumph dived to avoid detection. (2)

5 Sep 1939 At 0308 A/5, while patrolling between Egersund and Obrestad, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted a small destroyer bearing 140°, range 2.5 miles. Course of this vessel was 360°. This was considered to be a Norwegian torpedo boat on patrol. HMS Triumph dived to avoid detection. (2)
6 Sep 1939 At 2030 A/6, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) left patrol to return to Dundee. (1)
8 Sep 1939 Around 0625 A/8, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Dundee. (1)
23 Sep 1939 Around 1930 A/23, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) departed from Dundee for her 2nd war patrol. She is to patrol off the South coast of Norway.

24 Sep 1939 At 1550 A/24, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) suffered a rudder failure and the submarine had to be steered by the engines. Repairs were started and completed successfully at 0330 A/24.
25 Sep 1939 At 1527 A/25, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN), sighted a Tribal-class destroyer bearing 090°, steering 150°. Distance off track was 1500 yards.

At 1755 A/25, in position 57°29'N, 04°38'E, HMS Triumph sighted two Tribal class destroyers bearing 310°. One of the two destroyer altered course steering directly toward the submarine which then fired a smoke candle and dived to 120 feet. Communications by S.S./T. was established. One of the destroyers apparently was HMS Matabele (Cdr. G.K. Whitmy-Smith, RN) and the destroyers then both left the scene. (1)

27 Sep 1939 At 1400 A/27, while patrolling in the Skagerrak to the south-east of Kristiansand in approximate position 57°51'N, 08°36'E, sighted an enemy torpedo boat bearing 360°. Enemy course was 250°.

At 1700 A/27, in position 57°51'N, 08°35'E, an enemy torpedo boat was sighted bearing 210°. Enemy course was 290°. HMS Triumph dived to 90 feet and stopped all motors. It appeared that she was then hunted by two torpedo boats which were stopping to listen several times and then proceeding again. [There is no mention in the German records of these units which indicates that they were aware of or suspected the presence of an enemy submarine.]

The torpedo boat(s) was / were most likely to be coming out of the following; Iltis, Wolf, Seeadler and Leopard. These four torpedo boats were inspecting shipping in the Skagerrak together with the destroyers Z 10/Hans Lody, Z 14/Friedrich Ihn and Z 15/Erich Steinbrinck.
2202 A/27, Sighted a suspicious vessel and dived. Two diesel engined vessels then remained in the area for 50 minutes. These were thought to be hunting. (3)

28 Sep 1939 At 0725 A/28, in position 57°34'N, 08°15'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) was hunted by two MTBs or fast trawlers and escaped by bottoming at 320 feet at 0940 A/28. The enemy vessels still remained in the area though but no depth charges were dropped.
[We have been unable to find any enemy vessels fitting the description in the area.]
At 1200 A/28, HMS Triumph came to 70 feet and then left the area bottoming again at 205 feet at 1320 A/28.
At 1950 A/28, HMS Triumph surfaced. (1)

29 Sep 1939 At 0940 A/29, in approximate position 57°39'N, 07°24'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted a motor torpedo boat laying stopped. [We have been unable to place any enemy motor torpedo boats or motor minesweepers in the area.] (1)

30 Sep 1939 At 0230 A/30, near Lister, Norway, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) heard HE passing rapidly up the starboard side. This was considered to be either a torpedo or a motor torpedo boat, probably the latter. (1)

1 Oct 1939 At 2100 B/1, while patrolling in an area to the west of Egersund, Norway, the German U-boat U-16 sighted a submarine to the west at a range of about 4000 to 5000 meters. U-16 dived to close to investigate but the submarine could not be positively identified and as it was suspected that U-10 might be in the area no attack was made. [U-10 was actually no where near U-16.]

The submarine in question was most likely HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) which was patrolling in the area. Her 2000 A/1 [thus 2100 B/1] position was 58°32'N, 05°10'E. U-16 however does not give a grid position of this sighting. (4)

2 Oct 1939 At 2200 B/2, the German submarine U-16, which had been patrolling in an area to the west of Egersund and had commenced proceeding southwards to leave patrol not long before reported seeing a submarine bearing 310° at a range of about 1500 meters. U-16 dived and on surfacing at 0020 B/3 there was no sign of this submarine. [U-16 however does not give a grid position of this sighting.]

At 2220 A/2, [note the one hour time difference,], in position 250° - Obrestad Lighthouse - 8 miles, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) observed a track ahead which was thought to have been a splash on discharge and a torpedo track. HMS Triumph dived but could not confirm a submarine was present near her.
Possibly these sightings were related to each other. (4)

3 Oct 1939 At 2230 A/3, while patrolling to the west-north-west of Egersund, in approximate position 58°40'N, 05°20'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted a small darkened vessel. This was not considered to be a submarine. HMS Triumph dived to evade.
At 2258 A/3, a diesel engine was heard but nothing could be seen. Triumph's position was 275° - Obrestad lighthouse - 8 miles.
[No German submarine was in this area on this day.] (5)
7 Oct 1939 At 1200 A/7, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) left patrol to return to Dundee. (1)

8 Oct 1939 At 2150 A/8, about 80 nautical miles west of Aberdeen, a destroyer was sighted. HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) fired a smoke candle and dived, bottoming at 250 feet at 2205 A/8. No attack followed and the destroyer was not heard to be hunting.
At 2233 A/8, HMS Triumph surfaced in position 57°04'N, 00°16'E and proceeded. (1)
9 Oct 1939 Around 0755 A/9, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Dundee. (1)
14 Oct 1939 Around 0225 A/14, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) departed from Dundee for Rosyth where she arrived around 1010 A/14. (6)

22 Oct 1939 Around 1000 A/22, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) departed from Rosyth for her 3rd war patrol. She is to perform an anti-submarine patrol off the West coast of Scotland near St. Kilda. She is escorted by HMS Sturdy (Lt.Cdr. G.T. Cooper, RN) as far as Dunnet Head. They parted company at 0230 A/23. (1)
22 Oct 1939 For the daily positions of HMS Triumph during her 3rd war patrol see the map below.
4 Nov 1939 At 1503 A/4, the merchant vessel Brandon (British, 6668 GRT, built 1917) reported having been missed by a torpedo about 120 nautical miles to the west of the Hebrides.
The attack must have been the German submarine U-33 which attacked a merchant vessel with two torpedoes, which both missed, at 1520 B/4 in position 56°40'N 10°39'W.

The British submarines, HMS Trident (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN) and HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN), both on anti-Uboat patrols to the west of Ireland and the Hebrides respectively, were both ordered to investigate and try to intercept this enemy submarine which was expected to be southbound. Both sighted nothing though. (7)

11 Nov 1939 At 0800 A/11, in position 58°40'N, 06°15'W, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) makes rendezvous with the destroyer HMS Maori (Cdr. G.N. Brewer, RN), that is to escort HMS Triumph and HMS Trident (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN) back to Rosyth. (1)
12 Nov 1939 Around 1030 A/12 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Rosyth. (1)
23 Nov 1939 Around 2030 Z/23, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) departed from Rosyth for her 4th war patrol. She is to patrol off Stavanger, Norway.
24 Nov 1939 While on passage from Rosyth to her patrol area, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted several vessels.
In position 57°07'N, 01°40'E, two small merchant vessels of about 1200 tons were sighted steering east and west respectively.
In position 57°13'N, 02°00'E, one Danish ship of about 800 tons was sighted proceeding on course 250°.
In position 57°16'N, 02°10'E, a Dutch fishing trawler was sighted. [No times are mentioned in the patrol report nor in the logbook.] (1)
25 Nov 1939 At 0200 Z/25, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) arrived in her patrol area and commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 250° - Lister lighthouse - 15 miles.
Several vessels were seen during the day but it seems likely all were neutral vessels.
The HE of two vessels, probably trawlers, was heard to stop. Nothing could be sighted through the periscope though.
A 5000 tons merchant vessel passed at a range of 2 miles on bearing 270°. Course of this vessel was 180°.
The lights of two vessels were sighted after dark, one was making Lister lighthouse, the other one was leaving proceeding westwards. (1)

26 Nov 1939 While patrolling off Lister, Norway, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted an American merchant vessel passing her at a range of 1 miles. Course of the American vessel was 140°. (1)

27 Nov 1939 During the afternoon HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) was ordered to search for her damaged sister ship HMS Triad (Lt.Cdr. R.McC.P. Jonas, RN) and escorting the destroyers. She is unable to find her stricken sister. (1)

28 Nov 1939 At 1230 Z/28, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) finally finds her stricken sister vessel HMS Triad (Lt.Cdr. R.McC.P. Jonas, RN) and the destroyers HMS Inglefield (Capt. P. Todd, RN) and HMS Maori (Cdr. G.N. Brewer, RN) which were escorting the stricken submarine.
At 1700 Z/28, HMS Triumph lost contact owning to the bad weather and visibility.
HMS Triumph tried to regain contact the next day but was unable to do so. She was later ordered to resume patrol. (1)
29 Nov 1939 From 29 November to 6 December 1939, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) patrolled in the vicinity of position 270° - Jaederen Point - 4 miles (to the south of Stavanger).
During this period numerous unidentified merchant vessels and trawlers passed up and down the coast. (1)

1 Dec 1939 While patrolling in the vicinity of position 270° - Jaederen Point - 4 miles, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted a Norwegian ship of the Hoegh Carrier-class proceeding southwards.
An American ship of the Mauna Loa-class passed at at a range of half a mile proceeding on course 140°.
The Norwegian tanker Brasil (8130 GRT, built 1935) passed at a distance of 2 miles proceeding northwards. (1)

5 Dec 1939 While patrolling in the vicinity of position 270° - Jaederen Point - 4 miles, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted a Japanese ship of the Akagi Maru-class passing at a distance of about a mile proceeding on course 350°. (1)

6 Dec 1939 While patrolling in the vicinity of position 270° - Jaederen Point - 4 miles, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) sighted an unidentified merchant vessel of about 3000 tons passing her at a range of about 2 miles to the westwards. The vessel was proceeding southwards.
At 1545 Z/6, fast and clear HE was heard proceeding southwards. Nothing could be seen through the periscope as it was getting dark. It was thought to have been a motor torpedo boat.
At 1630 Z/6, HMS Triumph left patrol to return to Rosyth. (1)
8 Dec 1939 Around 1030 Z/8, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) ended her 4th war patrol at Rosyth. (1)
14 Dec 1939 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) is docked in the floating dock (AFD 3) at the Rosyth Dockyard. (8)
16 Dec 1939 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) is undocked. (8)
23 Dec 1939 Around 1415 Z/23, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) departed from Rosyth for her 5th war patrol. She is to patrol in the Skagerrak in zone C 2.
23 Dec 1939 In a signal timed 2317 Z/23 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.), HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) is ordered to patrol in position 56°44'N, 05°00'E. (1)

26 Dec 1939 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) hits a floating mine in the North Sea in position 56°44'N, 05°00'E. Her bow was badly damaged, but fortunately the torpedoes in the torpedo tubes did not detonate. Some eighteen feet of bow was blown off and her pressure hull was also damaged. The boat managed to limp back home under the protection of fighter aircraft and destroyers. HMS Triumph was under repair at Chatham Dockyard until 27 September 1940.

2300 Z/26 - The Officer of the Watch (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, RN) sighted a floating mine about 20 feet ahead. The mine was floating free and was first sighted on the crest of the swell. The wheel was put hard to starboard but Triumph had not started to swing when the mine exploded. The explosion was very heavy and there was a sheet of flame of about 50 feet high.
Damage was inspected and acted upon as far as possible. A signal was made to base that Triumph was returning and requesting escort. (1)
27 Dec 1939 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) is joined by an escort during her return to Rosyth.
0943 Z/27 - Sighted one Hudson aircraft.
0955 Z/27 - There were now four Hudson aircraft overhead.

1056 Z/27 - Sighted one enemy aircraft approaching. The Lewis gun was manned and the bridge was cleared with the exception of the Commanding Officer and the gun crews. The enemy was however driven off by the escorting Hudson aircraft.

1105 Z/27 - Sighted HMS Exmouth (Capt. R.S. Benson, DSO, RN), HMS Echo (Cdr. S.H.K. Spurgeon, DSO, RAN), HMS Electra (Lt.Cdr. S.A. Buss, MVO, RN), HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St.J. Morgan, RN) and HMS Escapade (Cdr. H.R. Graham, RN).
1110 Z/27 - The enemy aircraft again came in to attack but was again driven off by the Hudsons. (1)
28 Dec 1939 Around 1400 Z/28, the damaged HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) ended her 5th war patrol at Rosyth. (1)
29 Dec 1939 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) is docked in the floating dock (AFD 3) at the Rosyth Dockyard. (8)
6 Jan 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) is undocked. (9)
7 Jan 1940 Around 0845 Z/7, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) departed from Rosyth for Chatham dockyard where she will be repaired. She is escorted by HMS Exmouth (Cdr. R.S. Benson, DSO, RN). (9)
8 Jan 1940 Around 1800 Z/8, HMS Griffin (Lt.Cdr. J. Lee-Barber, RN) takes over the escort of HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) from HMS Exmouth (Cdr. R.S. Benson, DSO, RN).
9 Jan 1940 Around 1530 Z/9, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN) arrived at Chatham dockyard for repairs. (9)
30 Sep 1940 With her repairs and refit completed HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) proceeded from the Chatham Dockyard to Sheerness. On arrival compass adjustment trials were carried out. (10)

1 Oct 1940 Around 0625 A/1, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Sheerness for Harwich where she arrived around 1930 A/1. She was escorted by HMS Pintail (Lt.Cdr. T.H. Hill-Walker, RN). (11)

2 Oct 1940 Around 1230 A/2, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Harwich for Holy Loch. On departure D/G trials were carried out. She made part of the passage in a convoy until 0940 A/4 when she made rendezvous with her escort towards Holy Loch, HMS White Bear (Capt. R. Gill, RNR). (11)
This convoy departed Southend around 1000 A/2.

It was made up of the following merchant vessels; Alice Marie (British, 2206 GRT, built 1920), Chatwood (British, 2768 GRT, built 1929), Corcrest (British, 2373 GRT, built 1918), Corfirth (British, 1803 GRT, built 1934), Cormorant (British, 1220 GRT, built 1927), Dagenham (British, 2178 GRT, built 1919), Marlwood (British, 1034 GRT, built 1924), Pitwines (British, 889 GRT, built 1923), S.N.A. 8 (British (former French), 2569 GRT, built 1930), Sandenburgh (Dutch, 1013 GRT, built 1915), Sheaf Water (British, 2730 GRT, built 1925), Southwood (British, 1149 GRT, built 1937), Veerhaven (Dutch, 5291 GRT, built 1930) and Zelo (British, 2294 GRT, built 1921).
The Royal Fleet Auxiliary (RFA) tanker Larchol (1073 GRT, built 1917) was also part of the convoy.
On depature from Southend the convoy was escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Wolsey (Lt.Cdr. C.H. Campbell, DSC, RN) and HMS Valorous (Lt.Cdr. E. Mack, RN).

At 1600 A/2, the Larchol was detached to Harwich while the submarine HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) and her escort, the patrol vessel HMS Widgeon (Lt.Cdr. R. Frederick, RN), joined coming from Harwich.
At 0437 A/3, in position 53°05'N, 01°35'E, HMS Wolsey collided with an unknown trawler. There was only some very slight damage done to HMS Wolsey and apparently none to the trawler.
At 0921 A/3, the Pitwines was ordered to proceed to the Humber due to insufficient speed. She was to join a slower convoy later.

At 1020 A/3, the following merchant vessels joined coming from the Humber; Clan Lamont (British, 7673 GRT, built 1939) and Helmspey (British, 4764 GRT, built 1931) while the Marlwood was detached to the Humber.
At 1355 A/3, HMS Widgeon was detached to join southbound convoy FS 98 / 3rd series.
The Sandenburgh was detached to the Tees for Middlesbrough.
The Corfirth was detached to Hartlepool.
The Chatwood, Corcrest and S.N.A. 8 were detached to Blyth.
The Cormount, Sheaf Water, Southwood, Veerhaven and Zelo were detached to the Tyne.
The remainder of the convoy arrived off Methil around 1000 A/4. (12)
6 Oct 1940 Around 1330 A/6, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived at Holy Loch for a period of trials and training. (11)
9 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted exercises in Loch Long. (11)
10 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted exercises in Loch Long. Upon completion of these HMS Triumph proceeded to Arrochar. (11)
13 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (11)
14 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (11)
15 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) proceeded from Arrochar to Greenock. (11)
20 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) proceeded from Greenock to Holy Loch. (11)
21 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted mine discharge trials (from the torpedo tubes) in the Clyde area. (11)
22 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Arrochar. (11)
23 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (11)
24 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. Upon completion of these HMS Triumph proceeded to Holy Loch. (11)
29 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) served as the target. (11)
31 Oct 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (11)
1 Nov 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Holy Loch. (13)
2 Nov 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted D/G trials on the degaussing range off Helensburgh. (13)

6 Nov 1940 Around 0825 A/6, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed Holy Loch for Gibraltar. She made the southbound passage through the Irish Sea together with HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. H.F. Bone, RN). The submarines were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) until around 0330 A/8 when they parted company near Bishops Rock.
HMS Tigris had already parted company around 1345 A/7 afgter having developed engine trouble.
En-route a short anti-Uboat patrol was made making this HMS Triumph's 6th war patrol.
[No patrol report is available so some details for this patrol will be missing.] (14)
6 Nov 1940 For the daily positions of HMS Triumph during her 6th war patrol see the map below.
7 Nov 1940 In a signal timed 1303 A/7, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) ordered HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) to conduct an anti-Uboat patrol near position 47°30'N, 08°00'W. (15)
16 Nov 1940 Around 1515 A/16, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived at Gibraltar.
She was escorted in by HMS Kingston Turquoise (Lt. H.G. Chesterman, RNR) which had joined shortly before 0800 A/16. (14)
26 Nov 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is docked in No.3 graving dock at the Gibraltar Dockyard. (14)
28 Nov 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is undocked. (14)

30 Nov 1940 Around 1815 A/30, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Gibraltar for her 7th war patrol. This is an anti-submarine patrol in the Alboran Sea to the east of Gibraltar between 35°30'N / 35°55'N and 03°20'W / 02°40'W. HMS Triumph was ordered not to attack Vichy-French ships.
[No patrol report is available so some details for this patrol may be missing.]

6 Dec 1940 In signals timed 1102 A/6 and 1740 A/6 the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Leave patrol after dark today and return to Gibraltar. HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.F. de Salis, RN) will meet you during daylight 7/12 and provide escort. [The position of the rendezvous was included in the signal but is not listed in the war diary summaries.] (16)
7 Dec 1940 Around 1315 A/7, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 7th war patrol at Gibraltar.
She was escorted in by HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.F. de Salis, RN) which had joined at 1035 A/7 in position 35°51'N, 05°05'W. (1)
12 Dec 1940 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted attack and A/S exercises with destroyers off Gibraltar. (17)
14 Dec 1940 Around 1810 A/14, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Gibraltar for her 8th war patrol. This is another patrol in the Alboran Sea, to the south of the Alboran Islands.
22 Dec 1940 Around 1200 A/22, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 8th war patrol at Gibraltar.

1 Jan 1941 Around 2230 A/1, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Gibraltar for her 9th war patrol. She is to take up a patrol position with HMS Upholder (Lt. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) and HMS Usk (Lt.Cdr. P.R. Ward, RN) to the west of Gibraltar off Cape Spartel. This was most likely to intercept Vichy-French warships that might sortie from Casablanca to operate against Allied shipping as a reprisal for actions against Vichy-French convoys.
HMS Triumph is to patrol in position 270° - Cape Spartel - 5 nautical miles.
For the daily position(s) of HMS Triumph during this patrol see the map below.
2 Jan 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is recalled to Gibraltar where she arrived around 1730 A/2. (19)

3 Jan 1941 Around 1900 A/3, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Gibraltar for her 10th war patrol and subsequent passage to Malta. HMS Triumph was to provide cover for convoy 'Excess' to Malta.

5 Jan 1941 At 1920 A/5, while dived in position 37°03'N, 01°33'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) picked up HE on bearing 343° estimated to be at a range of 3000 yards. HMS Triumph altered course to 350° and dived to 90 feet.
At 1937 A/5, the HE faded out.
At 2012 A/5, HMS Triumph surfaced. There was nothing in sight.

[We have been unable to place an Italian (or Vichy-French) submarine in this area at this time. The closest submarine was HMS Upholder (Lt. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) which was in 37°00'N, 01°00'E at 2000 A/5 which is about 26 miles from the reported position by HMS Triumph.] (19)

6 Jan 1941 In a signal timed 1815 A/6, the Vice-Admiral Malta, sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) and HMS Upholder (Lt. M.D. Wanklyn, RN); 1) After leaving the eastern end of patrols at 1900 A/9 proceed through the following positions [not included in the signal as it is displayed in the war diary summaries] to position 180° - Filfla Island - 5 miles. 2) Adjust speed to arrived off Grand Harbour on 12 January, Triumph at 0700 A/12 and Upholder 0730 A/12. (20)

6 Jan 1941 At 0635 A/6, in approximate position 37°40'N, 03°50'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a vessel of about 3000 tons bearing 080°. Course of this vessel was 240°. HMS Triumph altered course to evade.
At 0704 A/6, the vessel, which was most likely Vichy-French en-route to Algiers, passed at a range of 5 miles. (19)

9 Jan 1941 At 2245 A/9, in approximate position 38°24'N, 10°20'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a vessel burning it's navigation lights bearing 030°. Course of the vessel was 250°. HMS Triumph altered course to intercept.
At 2350 A/9, the gun was manned.
At 0007 A/10, the vessel was identified as being Yugoslav and HMS Triumph broke off the 'attack' and continued. (19)

10 Jan 1941 In a signal timed 1909 A/10, the Vice Admiral Malta sent the following information / orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) and HMS Upholder (Lt. M.D. Wanklyn, RN); My 1815 A/6. HMS Bonaventure (Capt. H.G. Egerton, RN) and HMS Jaguar (Lt.Cdr. J.F.W. Hine, RN) may leave Malta southwards on 11 January proceeding direct from the north end of the searched channel to Pantellaria. Keep well clear to the northward. (21)

11 Jan 1941 At 2043 A/11, south of Sicily, in position 36°38'N, 13°22'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 350° proceeding on course 095°. HMS Triumph turned away and dived to 150 feet.
At 2130 A/11, HMS Triumph returned to periscope depth but nothing could be seen.
At 2143 A/11, HMS Triumph surfaced and continued passage to Malta proceeding in course 160°.
The submarine sighted must have been the Italian Fratelli Bandiera which sailed from Trapani at 1800 B/7 for a patrol on the meridian from 36°25'N, 12°40'E and returned to Trapani at 1000 B/13. (19)
12 Jan 1941 Around 0800 B/12, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived at Malta, thus ending her 10th war patrol. (19)
15 Jan 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (19)
18 Jan 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is undocked. (19)
19 Jan 1941 Around 1000 A/19, during an enemy air raid on Malta, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) was missed by a large bomb which fell about 40 yards on the port beam of the submarine.
Around 1220 A/19, during another enemy air raid on Malta, HMS Triump was again missed by a large bomb which fell about 60 yards on the port quarter of the submarine. (19)
28 Jan 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (22)

11 Feb 1941 Around 1300 A/11, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Malta for her 11th war patrol. She was to proceed to the Gulf of Salerno to pick up a group of airborne commando troops (participating in Operation Colossus, offsite link) near the mouth of the river Sele.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Triumph during this patrol see the map below.

12 Feb 1941 At 0545 A/12, well to the north of Cape Bon, Tunisia, in position 37°25'N, 11°13'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a darkened ship approximately 4000 yards on the starboard beam. HMS Triumph turned towards with the intention to attack as it was initially thought to may have been a submarine.
At 0550 A/12, the vessel was identified as a surface vessel, probably a small merchant ship. HMS Triumph dived and altered course away. HMS Triumph remained dived for the day at periscope depth.

The small Italian transports Costanza (582 GRT, built 1902) and Bombardiere (613 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the Italian motorboats Cotugno and Cicconetti sailed from Trapani at 2145 B/11 for Pantelleria [We do not have the time of their arrival]. IT is possible the vessel sighted was one of the above. (1)

13 Feb 1941 At 0003 A/13, west-north-west of Marettimo, in position 38°08'N, 11°30'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a darkened ship bearing 080° at a range of 6000 yards. The Officer of the Watch altered course away but when the Commanding Officer arrived on the bridge he decided to close and endeavour to identify the enemy.

At 0017 A/13, when the range was about 4000 yards the enemy vessel was identified as a warship, most probably a destroyer or torpedo boat. The enemy was zig-zagging and on a westerly course. HMS Triumph dived and took avoiding action. The enemy appeared to have come from the north coast of Sicily and his HE eventually faded out to the westward.
This was probably the Italian naval auxiliary Adriatico (1976 GRT, built 1931) which sailed from Trapani at 2140 B/12 and arrived at Cagliari at 1205 B/13.
At 0102 A/13, HMS Triumph surfaced and continued passage.
At 2022 A/13, HMS Triumph altered course after the Vice-Admital Malta's signal timed 1656 A/13 was received in which HMS Triumph was recalled. (1)
15 Feb 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) attacked an Italian convoy to the north of Pantelleria. Five torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.
Due to the strong escort it was not possible to close further. No counter attack developed following the failed attack.
The target has not been definitely identified. Although described as a merchant ship escorted by four destroyers (it was in the middle of the night) it could have been part of a convoy.

It is not possible that the ship sighted was from the convoy made up of the German transports Arcturus (2576 GRT, built 1937), Alicante (2140 GRT, built 1934) and Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Pegaso and Orsa which were returning to Naples after a trip to Tripoli. This convoy was off Ustica at 2343 B/14 and thus already well past the position of HMS Triumph.

A convoy made up of the Italian transports Silvia Tripcovich (2365 BRT, built 1925) and Amba Aradam (405 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonio Chinotto had sailed from Tripoli at 0425 B/14. They were expected to pass Pantelleria at 1500 B/15, therefore it is unlikely they were the ships sighted (We do not have a convoy report but we do have their sailing orders, and they were supposed to sail at 0400 B/15, so they were actually a little late). It seems unlikely, given the sailing orders, that this convoy was already to the north of Pantelleria at this time.

A convoy made up of the Italian transports Bainsizza (7933 GRT, built 1930), Sabaudia (1590 GRT, built 1911) and the Italian tanker Utilitas (5342 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Attilio Deffenu (3510 GRT, built 1929) and the torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori had sailed from Tripoli at 0530 B/13-0600 B/13. Utilitas, escorted by Missori, arrived at Palermo at 1930 B/15 while Bainsizza and Sabaudia, escorted by Attilio Deffenu, continued on and arrived at Naples at 1510 B/16. This convoy could well have been in the vicinity of Pantelleria at the time of the attack. [unfortunately we have no report of this convoy from the escort leader.]
2340 A/14 - Sighted San Leonardo lighthouse flashing bearing 090°, distance 12 nautical miles.
2349 A/14 - Sighted a merchant vessel of about 6000 tons escorted by four destroyers. Three of the destroyers were ahead of the merchant vessel and one was astern. Started attack.

0018 A/15 - Fired five torpedoes from 5000 yards. It was intended to fire four torpedoes but due to an error in drill the tube space crew thought they were to fire six. However one (No.2 tube) misfired resulting in eventually five torpedoes to be fired.
0024 A/15 - Resumed passage back to Malta. (1)
16 Feb 1941 Around 0730 A/18, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 11th war patrol at Malta. (1)
20 Feb 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (23)
21 Feb 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is undocked.
23 Feb 1941 Around 1710 A/23, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Malta for her 12th war patrol. She is to patrol off the coast of Calabria in area 'M'.
On departure a practice attack was carried out on HMS Jade (?). (1)
23 Feb 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Triumph during her 12th war patrol see the map below.

23 Feb 1941 At 1843 A/23, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) received the Cdr. S's signal timed 0915 A/23 which reported that a convoy had left Palermo P.M./21 bound for Tripoli and that another convoy would leave Naples for Tripoli P.M./23. Cdr. Woods decided to patrol off Cape Murro di Porco until 1000 A/25 to intercept the 'Naples-convoy' in case it should proceed through the Straits of Messina.

This 'Naples-convoy' was made up of the German transports Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937), Marburg (7564 GRT, built 1928), Kybfels (7764 GRT, built 1937) and Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Geniere and the Italian torpedo boat Castore. This convoy sailed from Naples at 1730 B/23-1755 B/23. Castore turned back during the night and arrived at Naples at 1020 B/24. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1921 B/25-2050 B/25.

The 'Palermo-convoy' had sailed at 0805 B/21 and consisted of Italian transports Silvia Tripcovich (2365 GRT, built 1925) and Sabbia (5788 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari via Trapani (1810 B/21-1930 B/21). Sabbia was torpedoed by HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) at 1705 B/22 in position 35°47'N, 11°16'E, but was towed by Silvia Tripcovich and arrived at Tripoli at 1316 B/24. (1)
26 Feb 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) attacked but missed the Italian transport Giovanni Boccaccio (3116 GRT, built 1919) off Cape dell'Armi near position 37°52'N, 15°39'E.

The Giovanni Boccaccio had sailed from Gallipoli at 0735 B/25 for Messina and Syracuse. [Despite a thorough search, we cannot find the Italian document which mentions the attack though we must have it in our possession, so we must rely on some notes regarding this attack.] ?

0811 A/26 - Sighted smoke from the direction of Cape Spartivento. The target was steering westwards and was thought to be a merchant vessel of 5000 tons. Started attack. Closed to a range of 2700 yards and at .....

0858 A/26 - Near position 37°52'N, 15°39'E fired two torpedoes. About four minutes after firing the target was seen to alter course about 45° to starboard and a large puff of smoke was seen to emerge from her funnel. Shortly afterwards she resumed course towards Messina. It was thought the target, that was now thought to be of 3000 tons, was able to evade the torpedoes, no explosions were heard. A small patrol vessel was seen near the target but it did not search for the attacker.

1642 A/26 - In position 37°36'N, 15°43'E, well to the south of Cape dell'Armi, a merchant vessel was seen steering a course of 350°. Subsequently a dummy attack was carried out on Italian hospital ship of about 10000 tons.
This was the Italian Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which had sailed from Tripoli at 1750 B/25 and arrived at Naples at 1310 B/27. (1)

1 Mar 1941 At 0831 A/1, while in approximate position 38°55'N, 17°18'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a north-east bound merchant ship bearing 290°, hull down, three miles to the east of Cape Colonne. An attack was not possible. Enemy course was 045°.

This may have been the Italian transport Rina Corrado (5180 GRT, built 1918) which sailed from Messina for Taranto at 1900 B/28 escorted by the Italian auxiliary D 23 / Lago Zuai (783 GRT, built 1940) until Punta Stalletti. Rina Corrado was diverted to Crotone and sailed from there at 1810 B/2 escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Egitto (3329 GRT, built 1928), this time for Bari instead of Taranto. They arrived at Bari at 1450 B/3.

At 1738 A/1, near Cape Colonne, a two-masted schooner under sail was sighted bearing 325°. Enemy course was 140°. At 1751 A/1 this was was identified as being possibly an enemy auxiliary A/S schooner.

The schooner sighted at 1738 A/1 may have been either the V 37 / Adua (148 GRT, built 1907) or V 27 / Faccetta Nera (?) which both sailed from Gallipoli at 0835 B/1 for a patrol and returned at 1415 B/2. (24)
2 Mar 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) tries to attack an Italian armed merchant cruiser near Capo Cimiti. The attack however had to be broken off.

The armed merchant cruiser was most likely the Italian Egitto (3329 GRT, built 1927). Which was escorting Italian transport Valdirosa (4434 GRT, built 1914). They had sailed from Naples at 1640 B/28 with the Italian tanker Portofino (6424 GRT, built 1916) which was detached to Messina at about 1800 B/1 for eventual passage to Augusta. Valdirosa was detached to Crotone where she arrived at about noon/2. She sailed from Crotone at 1810 B/2 for Taranto. Egitto joined the Italian transport Rina Corrado (5180 GRT, built 1918) [see the event for 1 March 1941] off Crotone and escorted her to Bari, arriving at 1515 B/3.
Shortly afterwards three torpedoes were fired at a merchant ship of about 3000 tons. All torpedoes fired missed their target.

The ships in the convoy attacked were the Italian Leonardo Palomba (1110 GRT, built 1899) and Irma Calzi (668 GRT, built 1907) They had sailed from Messina at 1750 B/1 and arrived at Crotone at 1330 B/2 for eventual passage to Brindisi and Durazzo, respectively.

0858 A/2 - While deep for an aircraft HE was heard. Upon coming to periscope depth sighted a laden merchant vessel of about 5000 tons and what looked like an armed yacht well to the seaward of the merchant vessel. Started attack.

It soon proved to be impossible to attack the merchant vessel but as it was seen that the escort was bigger then was first thought an attack on that vessel was started. The attack however failed due to problems with bringing the torpedo tubes to the ready.
1019 A/2 - Sighted two ships close inshore off Capo Cimiti. The first was a 1200 tons coaster, the second, half a mile astern, was a loaded ship of about 3000 tons. Started attack.

1040 A/2 - Near position 38°53'N, 17°15'E fired three torpedoes at a 3000 tons merchant ship from 2000 yards. But as Triumph was still turning slightly during firing all torpedoes most likely missed ahead. (1)
4 Mar 1941 At 1315 A/4, to the west of Cape Spartivento, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a motor torpedo boat well inshore.
This was possibly either the Italian MAS 526 or MAS 518 which both had sailed from Messina at 0720 B/4 for a patrol and returned at 1815 B/4. (25)

5 Mar 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant vessels Marzamemi (958 GRT, built 1891) and Colomba Lofaro (897 GRT, built 1890) near Capo dell'Armi, Calabria, Italy position 37°54'N, 15°46'E.

These ships had sailed from Catania on the morning of 5 March for Torre Viscosa (Porto Buso) via Crotone. As a consequence of the attack, the German transport Samos (2576 GRT, built 1923) on passage from Bari to Naples took refuge in Crotone. Colomba Lofaro was the first ship sunk.
According to Italian sources four men were killed and four were wounded.

0709 A/5 - Sighted a convoy of four merchant vessels of about 2000 to 3000 tons rounding Cape Spartivento and subsequently Cape dell'Armi while keeping one mile offshore. Speed of the convoy was 8 knots. The convoy was escorted by an armed merchant cruiser of the Citta di Genova-class. Also an armed trawler was present. An attack was started on the armed merchant cruiser but it was broken off at 0853 A/5 and no torpedoes were fired.

This was most probably the convoy made up of the Italian transports Zeffiro (5165 GRT, built 1918), Persiano (2474 GRT, built 1889), Liv (3068 GRT, built 1896) and Etruria (2633 GRT, built 1906) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Città di Tunisi (5419 GRT, 1929) which sailed from Bari at 1500 B/3 for Messina and was expected to pass Crotone at 1900 B/4. [The time of arrival of theconvoy at Messina is unknown to us.]

0935 A/5 - Sighted a three-funnelled ship, later identified as the Vichy-French Providence (11196 GRT, built 1920) of which HMS Triumph was aware. The vessel kept well away from the land and was eventually met by a trawler five mile from Cape dell'Armi and then led towards Messina.

The trawler met by the Providence was the Italian auxiliary R 53 / Lago Tana (47 GRT, built 1937) which had sailed from Messina at 0905 B/5 and piloted her through the Straits of Messina. Providence had sailed from Beirut on 1 March and arrived at Marseille on 8 March.

1218 A/5 - Sighted a merchant ship just clear of Cape dell'Armi. This vessel was eastbound and in ballast. Carried out a dummy attack as this ship was rather small, 2500 tons at most and was in ballast. Cdr. Woods decided to wait for bigger / better prey.

The only possible match we could find was the Italian transport Ninuccia (4583 GRT, built 1910) which sailed from Messina for Crotone and Taranto. No departure time is known to us but she was expected in the Crotone area at about midnight. The vessel however does not match description Cdr. Woods give.

1407 A/5 - Sighted a ship approaching from beyond Cape Dell'Armi. Shortly afterwards sighted the masts of a second ship astern of the other. Both ships were of about 2500 tons and were fully laden. Closed the shore to attack.

Due to a heavy rain squall both ships were lost out of sight for a considerable while. When the rain cleared the first ship was seen to be 3000 yards away and was now heading for the shore. The second ship was soon seen to join her. Both ships were later seen to drop their anchor off Melito di Porto Salvo.

1512 A/5 - Fired one torpedo against the first target from 1400 yards. The torpedo hit the target and as a result of the explosion the other ship was out of sight for several minutes. When seen again she had slipped her anchor and was seen to make off. At 1517 hours one torpedo was fired from 1500 yards but missed. The crew were then seen to abandon ship so when she was stopped, at 1534 hours, another torpedo was fired from 1000 yards which hit. She was seen to sink soon after.
HMS Triumph proceeded eastwards along the coast until 1645 A/5 after which course was altered to seaward to clear the area. (1)
6 Mar 1941 At 0640 A/6, near Punta Stilo, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a northbound merchant vessel bearing 358° which kept about a mile from the shore. An attack was not possible.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel, unfortunately the Italian records of Maricotraf from Supermarina have a few pages missing during this period].
At 2316 A/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2001 A/6 was received which ordered HMS Triumph to leave patrol at 1200 A/8. Cdr. Woods decided to spend the next day in the Rizzuto - Colonne area. (24)

7 Mar 1941 At 0507 A/7, to the north-east of Crotone, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a white topsail schooner running on an auxiliary motor. Range was 500 yards. Lt.Cdr. Woods considered gun action but decided not to do so as he considered it not worth to disclose the submarines position. Also the schooner, likely to be an A/S schooner, may work in cooperation with a submarine of motor torpedo boat.

At 0530 A/7, a green topsail schooner was sighted at a range of 2000 yards. This schooner was seen to signal the first schooner sighted. So now considered it indeed very likely this were A/S schooners. Avoiding action was taken and HMS Triumph withdrew to seaward until clear at 0815 A/8 when course was altered for Cape Colonne.
At 1330 A/7, HMS Triumph sighted what appeared to be the bridge and funnel of a destroyer or torpedo boat just clear of Rizzutto. This shortly afterwards disappeared behind the cape.

At 1400 A/7, what appeared to be a submarine was seen on the surface. The vessel was northbound. HMS Triumph turned in to attack but the object was soon identified as a motor torpedo boat. This may have been the same object seen half an hour before. Shortly afterwards both auxiliary schooners were seen proceeding to the north-east alond the 100 fathom line, zig-zagging widely. The motor torpedo boat (or motor A/S boat) remained stopped so as to listen. Cdr. Woods considered gunning this vessel at dusk but decided not to do so unless the enemy would follow HMS Triumph.
then withdrew to seaward to get well clear of the area before surfacing. (1)
7 Mar 1941 At 1100 A/7, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) which had been patrolling near Punta Stilo, left patrol to proceed to Alexandria.
At 2107 A/7, HMS Triumph passed out of her patrol area. (1)
13 Mar 1941 Around 0830 B/13, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 12th war patrol at Alexandria. (1)
18 Mar 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is docked in the Gabbari graving dock at Alexandria. (25)
21 Mar 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is undocked. (25)
24 Mar 1941 Around 1000 B/24, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Alexandria for her 13th war patrol.

The object of this patrol was the reconnaissance of certain beaches in the Dodecanese. This was to be achieved by Triumph approaching the selected landing places in the dark and landing a party by Folbot canoe after the landing placed had been examined by periscope observation during the day.
26 Mar 1941 In the early morning hours HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived off Rhodes. During the forenoon periscope reconnaissance was made.

After dark Triumph returned to the area to make a landing by Folbot canoe. Shortly before midnight, when about to launch the Folbot, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2205 A/26 was received which contained the following orders; Cancel previous orders. Proceed forthwith with utmost despatch to position 36°10'N, 26°25'E.
This was due to the Italian battle fleet being reported at sea and it was thought the enemy might well enter the Aegean to intercept British convoys between Alexandria and Piraeus. (26)

27 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 0027 B/27, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) and HMS Rover (Lt.Cdr. G.H. Greenway, RN); My 2205 B/26 to Triumph and my 2209 B/26 to Rover.. Proceed with utmost despatch to establish offensive patrol by dawn 28/3 as follows; Triumph within 20 miles of position 180° - Anti Milo - 20 miles, routed through positions 36°10'N, 26°25'E and 36°18'N, 25°17'E. Rover within 20 miles of Cape Drepano, Suda Bay by proceeding through positions 33°10'N, 23°25'E and 35°00'N, 22°45'E, thence through the Kithera Channel.

In a signal timed 1125 B/27, these submarines were informed by the Capt. S.1 of the composition of the enemy forces approaching the Aegean from the westward. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]

In a signal timed 2315 B/27, the Capt. S.1 informed the submarines of the movements of the British forces involved in the operation against the Italian battlefleet. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (27)
28 Mar 1941 Around 0300 B/28, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived in her ordered patrol area south of Anti-Milos. (1)

28 Mar 1941 In a signal timed 1919 B/28, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) and HMS Rover (Lt.Cdr. G.H. Greenway, RN); Cancel my 0027 B/27. Triumph proceed forthwith routed north of Cristiani and Soorrana Island to comply with the initial patrol orders. Rover remain at present patrol area eastward of 24°30'E during the night proceeding on the surface to arrive off Suda Bay at 0700 A/29. Proceed into Suda Bay and act under orders of HMS York (Capt. R.H. Portal, DSC, RN). (27)
30 Mar 1941 In the early morning hours, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is back off Rhodes. Once again in the forenoon periscope reconnaissance was made.
After noon Triumph retired to seaward to charge.
After dark Triumph returned to the landing area.

31 Mar 1941 Shortly after midnight, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) launched a Folbot, manned by Commander Willmott, RN and Lt. Courtney, K.R.R.C. (King's Royal Rifle Corps), for a reconnaissance. After four hours the canoe was picked up and Triumph proceeded seaward.
During the afternoon periscope reconnaissance of another landing beach is carried out. Triumph withdrew to seaward again upon completion of this.
After dark Triumph returned to this new landing beach to launch a Folbot [Part of Operation 'Cordite']. (1)

1 Apr 1941 Shortly after midnight, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) launched a Folbot, manned by Commander Willmott, RN and Lt. Courtney, K.R.R.C. (King's Royal Rifle Corps), for a reconnaissance of the second landing beach. After 3 hours and 45 minutes the canoe was picked up and Triumph proceeded seaward. (1)
2 Apr 1941 During the forenoon, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) periscope reconnaissance was made of three other landing beaches. After completing this Triumph retired to seaward.
Half an hour before midnight a Folbot was launched to reconnoitre one of the landing beaches [Part of Operation 'Cordite']. (1)
3 Apr 1941 At 0330 B/3, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) picked up the canoe again. Triumph now set course to make a periscope reconnaissance of yet another landing beach.
At 0846 B/3, Triumph grounded at 34 feet. While trying to get off the rocks Triumph broke surface. She quickly retired from the area and dived again shortly afterwards.
As it seemed likely Triumph was detected it was decided to abandon any further landings and to return to Alexandria. (1)
5 Apr 1941 Around 0830 B/5, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 13th war patrol at Alexandria. (1)

15 Apr 1941 Around 1300 C/15, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Alexandria to participate in a raid on Bardia. The raiding force includes the antiaircraft cruiser HMS Coventry (Capt. D. Gilmour, RN) and the Australian destroyers HMAS Stuart (Capt. H.M.L. Waller, DSO, RAN), HMAS Voyager (Cdr. J.C. Morrow, DSO, RAN), HMAS Waterhen (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, RAN) bringing elements of Layforce (offsite link). The submarine carries an SBS section led by Captain Roger Courtney and to act as a beacon in position 013° - Bardia – 2.5 miles.
For the daily positions of HMS Triumph during these operations see the map below.

16 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 1759 C/16, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); On completion of operation return to Alexandria through position 32°02'N, 25°45'E to enter the sanctuary at 1100 C/18. (29)

16 Apr 1941 The infantery landing ships HMS Glenearn (Capt.(Retd.) L.B. Hill, OBE, RN) and HMS Glengyle (A/Capt.(Retd.) C.H. Petrie, RN) departed Alexandria around 0245 C/16 for a raid against Bardia. They were being escorted by the AA cruiser HMS Carlisle (Capt. T.C. Hampton, RN) and the destroyers HMAS Stuart (Capt. H.M.L. Waller, DSO, RAN), HMAS Voyager (Cdr. J.C. Morrow, DSO, RAN) and HMS Wryneck (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H.D. Lane, RN).
The submarine HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) was to act as beacon to guide the landing force in to the beach.
After dark on the 16th HMS Carlisle parted company to join convoy AN 27. She was replaced at daylight on the 17th by HMS Coventry (Capt. D. Gilmour, RN).
The raid however was cancelled due to the bad weather conditions and the force returned to Alexandria around 1300 C/17. (30)
18 Apr 1941 During the afternoon, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) briefly returns to Alexandria. She departed again for the Bardia area after a few hours. (28)

19 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 1710 C/19, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); On completion of operation return to Alexandria [the route was included in the signal but the route is not included in the signal as listed in the war diary summaries.] to enter the Sanctuary at about 1100 C/21. (29)

19 Apr 1941 The infantery landing ship HMS Glengyle (A/Capt.(Retd.) C.H. Petrie, RN) departed Alexandria around 0245 C/19 for a raid against Bardia. She was escorted by the AA cruiser HMS Coventry (Capt. D. Gilmour, RN) and the destroyers HMAS Stuart (Capt. H.M.L. Waller, DSO, RAN), HMAS Voyager (Cdr. J.C. Morrow, DSO, RAN) and HMAS Waterhen (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, RN). This last destroyer joined at sea as she had been on other duties before.
The submarine HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) acted as beacon to guide the landing force in to the beach.

Commandoes were landed near Bardia during the night of 19/20 March 1941. Operations commenced around 2205 C/19 and were completed around 0500 C/20 when course was set to return to Alexandria where they arrived around 2200 C/20. (31)
21 Apr 1941 Around 1200 C/21, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) returns to Alexandria. (28)
24 Apr 1941 Around 1945 C/24, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Alexandria for her 14th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Sirte.

25 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 1909 C/25, the Capt. S.1's informed HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) of the following; Aircraft reconnaissance P.M.24 reported one 240 foot motor vessel under way in the northern basin of Benghazi harbour. One 150 foot vessel at end of the northern breakwater. One 5000 ton merchant ship 12 miles offshore heading towards Benghazi. (29)
27 Apr 1941 At 2224 C/27, in position 32°38'N, 19°39'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a light to the southward on bearing 170°.
At 2334 C/27, the light was identified as being from a hospital ship which was on the direct Benghazi - Messina route proceeding on course 330°.
The hospital ship sighted was the Italian Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which had departed Benghazi around 1705 B/27. She arrived at Naples on the 29th (time unknown). (32)

28 Apr 1941 At 1120 C/28, when in approximate position 32°12'N, 19°57'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a 5000 tons merchant vessel approaching Benghazi on course 070° with either an escort or a small coaster ahead. There was no chance of an attack.
The larger ships subsequently berthed in the inner harbour and the smaller in the outer harbour.

The only shops recorded to have arrived at Benghazi more or less around this time were the small motor vessel Rosa (559 GRT, built 1913) and the schooners Rosina (44 GRT, built 1937), Giorgina (253 GRT, built 1918) and Unione (216 GRT, built 1908). They had sailed from Tripoli at 1810 B/23 escorted by the torpedo boat Climene as far as Sirte and arrived at Benghazi at 1400 B/28-1445 B/28.
In the first half of the afternoon HMS Triumph conducted a periscope reconnaissance of the harbour from a position 2.5 miles from the breakwater.
At 1855 C/28, a 3000 tons merchant vessel was seen to leave the harbour. This vessel was followed by a large three-masted barque. Enemy course was about 240°. No attack was possible.
At 1930 C/28, a 5000 tons merchant vessel departed the harbour. She was followed about 20 minutes later by an armed liner of about 3000 tons. Again it was not possible to attack.

Departures from Benghazi are recorded as follows; The Italian auxliary (former passenger ship looking like a liner) Città di Bari (3339 GRT, built 1928) sailed from Benghazi at 1845 B/28 and arrived at Tripoli at 0700 B/30. The transport Regulus (1085 GRT, built 1884) sailed from Benghazi at 1915 B/28 and arrived at Tripoli at 1100 B/2 May.

As far as could be seen this left no targets in the outer harbour although the 5000 tons merchant vessel was still in the inner harbour. It had originally been intended to launch a Folbot that night for an attack on shipping in the harbour but Lt.Cdr. Woods now decided to set off in pursuit of the ships seen leaving the harbour until 0200 C/29 at latest.
At 2100 C/28, HMS Triumph surfaced and set off in pursuit on course 240°. It was hoped that the rear ships could be overtaken between 2300 C/28 and 2400 C/28.
Two signals were received in the second half of the evening;

The Capt. S.1's signal timed 1909 C/28 [or 1919 C/28, sources give two different time of origins for this signal] stated the following; Photographic analysis of 27 April shows one 340 foot ship alongside the outer end of the north-east breakwater with lighters alongside. Entrance to northernmost inner harbour is partially blocked by two ships as follows. 5000-ton ship lying head north-east stem close to outer end of inner mole. A 2000-ton ship lying head north, stern touching bow of 5000-ton ship. There is a gap of 300 feet between north-east breakwater and the bow of the second ship.
The Cdr. S.1's signal (Senior Officer Submarines Malta) timed 2013 B/18, stated the following; Believe three transports loaded with munitions and petroleum are due at Benghazi on 29 April.
At 0001 C/29, the chase was broken off as there was no sign of the enemy ships seen earlier and HMS Triumph set course to return to Benghazi. (33)

30 Apr 1941 At 1349 C/30, when in approximate position 32°04'N, 19°48'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a large three-masted barque close inshore proceeding towards Benghazi. This vessel could not be attacked and entered the harbour around 1530 C/30.
This was most likely the Italian G 19 / Giorgio (196 GRT, built 1912).
G 19 / Giorgio (196 GRT, built 1912) had sailed from Tripoli at 1250 B/26 with the schooners Rosa (246 GRT, built 1922?) and Igea (158 GRT, built 1917).
Rosa arrived at Benghazi 1050 B/30, Giorgio at 1445 B/30 and Igea at 1720 B/1 May.
Lt.Cdr. Woods decided not to send in the Folbot this night as most likely the barque was the only vessel in the harbour and her wanted to keep the 'surprise' till later on a more decent target. (1)

1 May 1941 At 0728 C/1, when in approximate position 32°00'N, 19°48'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a 2500-ton merchant ship, a large three-masted barque and a small motor coaster approaching Benghazi.
HMS Triumph closed the harbour entrance submerged at 6 knots but was unable to get into an attack position as the range could not be closed to less than 7000 yards.

At 0828 C/1, the steamer was seen to enter harbour, followed by the schooner. Lt.Cdr. Woods decided to continue to patrol off Benghazi in case the steamer would leave at dusk and if she did not attack it in the harbour with the use of the Folbot and sabotage party. The steamer however berthed in the inner harbour and Lt.Cdr. Woods considered gunning her but decided against it as the target would be difficult to hit as it was screened by wrecks.
[No ships are recorded to have entered Benghazi around this time.]
At 1131 C/1, a sharp explosion was heard but this appeared to have nothing to do with HMS Triumph.
This was most likely a magnetic mine which was being blown up near the harbour entrance around this time.
At 1645 C/1, the steamer was seen to be on the move so Lt.Cdr. Woods closed her expected track in case she would leave the harbour but she did not do so and was apparently only shifting berth.

At 1724 C/1, a large fire, with heavy black smoke was seen in the north corner of the inner harbour. This was most likely the Serdica which was under air attack. She was hit and blew up with debris starting fires in the harbour and also on the shore.
At 1735 C/1, a small motor drifter arrived passing 500 yards from HMS Triumph. She was not attacked as the steamer would be a much better target in case she was coming out.
At 2023 C/1, the three-masted barque and three small motor coasters were seen to leave the harbour, keeping too close inshore to allow HMS Triumph to get withing effective gun range.
This were the G.19 / Giorgio and Rosa leaving for Tripoli. Apparently two other vessels were in company.
In the evening Lt.Cdr. Woods decided not to deploy the Folbot as the steamer was in a very difficult position to attack. He decided to patrol in the Gulf of Sirte instead. (34)

1 May 1941 In a signal timed 1451 C/1, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders / information to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Concentrate on Benghazi. Following is latest information. There is probably no coast defence artillery at the port. Consideration should be given attacking ships in harbour with gunfire. (35)

2 May 1941 At 1400 C/2, when off Zuwetina, in approximate position 31°05'N, 19°55'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a petrol or water carrier close inshore about 1.5 miles to the north of Zuwetina.
At 1405 C/2, HMS Triumph surfaced for gun action but on surfacing it was seen that the target was ashore and abandoned.
Perhaps the vessel seen was the wreck of the small tanker Rosanna (Italian, 205 GRT, built 1921) which foundered off Zuetina on 9 February 1941.

Zuwetina appeared to be deserted. It was Lt.Cdr. Woods intention to fire a few rounds at a small fort to the south of the village to find out but the first round misfired and the attempt was abandoned. HMS Triumph subsequently retired to seaward until clear of the 20 fathom line. (1)

3 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary patrol vessel V 136 / Tungin F. (425 GRT, built 1919) with gunfire off Marsa el Brega, Libya in position in position 30°27'N, 19°32'E.

The Tungin F. had arrived at Benghazi from Tripoli on 11 April. Italian sources do not record her departure from Benghazi, however, the German KTB indicates it was on 20 April. We assume she was on the return trip from Benghazi to Tripoli when she was sunk but it is not clear why she was still at Marsa el Brega on this day.
1128 C/3 - Surfaced to close two schooners sighted before. One of them was now seen to be a wreck so it was decided to attack the other one with gunfire.
1202 C/3 - Opened fire from 2500 yards. After firing 5 rounds it was seen that this schooner was also a wreck so fire was ceased at 1205 hours.

The wrecks seen may have been from the schooner V 85 / Teresuccia (313 GRT, built 1920) and the tug G 14 / Tenax (115 GRT, built 1930), which had both been scuttled off Marsa El Brega on 9 February 1941.
1225 C/3 - Sighted masts of a small coaster ahead. Closed to attack.

1248 C/3 - Opened fire but ceased fire two minutes later as the crew of the vessel had meanwhile was abandoning ship. Closed the boats and two of the crew were taken prisoner. Now closed and boarded the coaster and took some charts and papers.
Returned the two crew to their boats and opened fire on the coaster at 1357 C/3. The coaster was seen to be hit but did not sink before Triumph was forced to dive for an approaching aircraft.
1426 C/3 - Surfaced to finish off the damaged coaster.
1428 C/3 - Dived for an approaching aircraft. Did not surface again as at 1435 C/3 the coaster was seen to sink. HMS Triumph subsequently retired to seaward. (1)
6 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) makes a torpedo attack on an enemy convoy in the Gulf of Sirte near position 31°30'N, 15°54'E. Four torpedoes were fired by no hits were obtained.
The convoy sighted was made up of the German transports Tilly M. Russ (1600 GRT, built 1926) and Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Climene.

The convoy sailed from Tripoli for Benghazi at 1300 B/5. Following the submarine attack, the convoy turned back as ordered by MariLibia and proceeded to Buerat el Sun where they anchored at 2030 B/6. There was a strong wind during the following day and the convoy sailed again at 1930 B/8. At 1008 B/9, off El Auegia, they were informed that an enemy submarine had been spotted 3 miles off El Auegia and the convoy was ordered by Marilibia to return to Buerat el Sun and were informed that the torpedo boat Partenope would meet them. Partenope joined them and told them to proceed to Tripoli where they arrived at 1432 B/10.
Climene counter attacked and dropped several depth charges but many failed to explode due to the shallow water (30-35 meters).
1420 C/6 - Sighted a 500 tons motor coaster and two merchant vessels of 3000 tons leaving Burat. They were escorted by a Spica-class destroyer. Started attack.

1449 C/6 - Though not in a very good position for attack fired four torpedoes as it was considered most important to disrupt this coastal traffic. Range was 3600 yards. No hits were obtained. Two torpedo explosions were heard, most likely they were exploding on the beach. The escort began searching near the firing position and dropped two depth charges but by that time Triumph was about 2500 yards from that position.

1550 C/6 - The escort disappeared in the direction of the convoy. HMS Triumph withdrew to seaward to get into deeper water to reload. She then set course to comply with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1555 C/5 which had contained the following orders; Keep to southward of latitude 31°20'N from 0800 C/7 until 1200 C/8. Leave patrol P.M./8 to arrive in the Alexandria Sanctuary at 0500 C/12, routed along Cyrenaican coast as far as 23°08'E. Then through position 32°38'N, 25°00'E, thence direct to the Sanctuary. If time permits spend daylight 10th off Apollonia.

In a signal, HMS Triumph's 1647 C/6 informed the Capt. S.1 and Cdr. S.1 (Senior Officer Submarines Malta) of the following; Three merchant ships escorted by one destroyer left Burat 1430 C/6, course 100° and speed 8 knots. (36)

7 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) fired three torpedoes against an enemy merchant vessel in the Gulf of Sirte near position 30°59'N, 17°59'E. This may have been the Italian merchant vessel Silvio Scaroni (1394 GRT, built 1920) carrying stores from Benghazi to Tripoli.
The Silvio Scaroni had sailed from Benghazi at 1915 B/6 and arrived at Tripoli at 1300 B/9.

0649 C/7 - Dived 6 miles west of El Auegia (Uwayja), intending to patrol eastwards along the coast all day if nothing was sighted. On approaching El Auegia a 500-ton schooner, a 250-ton brigantine and a 100-ton motor coaster were seen in the anchorage.
The vessels sighted were possibly the schooners Igea (158 GRT, built 1917) and Rosa (246 GRT, built 1922), which had sailed from Benghazi at 1900 B/2 and arrived at Tripoli at 1200 B/12.
Lt.Cdr. Woods decided to patrol off the entrance to the anchorage and attack with the gun shortly after sunset.
1400 C/7 - The schooner and motor coaster left the anchorage and turned westwards. Both were light.

1424 C/7 - When just about to surface a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen approaching from the westward at high speed. This torpedo boat covered the passage of the two vessels until they were past Ras el Auegua and HMS Triumph was forced to seaward to evade this torpedo boat.

The torpedo boat subsequently continued eastwards at 16 knots leaving the two vessels unescorted but by the time the torpedo boat was out of sight the two vessels were 4 to 5 miles away. Lt.Cdr. Woods decided not to pursue but to wait for the brigantine to leave the anchorage.

The torpedo boat was most likely the Castore which sailed from Tripoli at 2107 B/6 at 20 knots to meet the schooner Frieda (246 GR, built 1918) and the tug Nettuno (406 GRT, built 1921) which were arriving from Benghazi (Frieda actually sailed from Benghazi only at 1900 B/8). Castore could not locate them and returned to Tripoli at 1944 B/9.
1745 C/7 - Sighted a 4000-ton merchant vessel in ballast approaching from the east. Started attack.

1814 C/7 - Fired three torpedoes from 1000 yards. When the periscope was raised again when the second torpedo fired should have hit it was seen that the target was squarely across the torpedo tracks. The wake of the third torpedo was seen approaching the stern of the ship. It soon appeared to graze the rudder but no explosion followed. Two torpedoes exploded on the beach. Triumph turned to bring some more torpedo tubes to bear but the target altered course and began firing her gun. Lt.Cdr. Woods did not surface to engage her with the gun as there was quite a swell and the gun of the enemy was already manned and firing. Also the torpedo boat seen earlier may turn up later. (1)

8 May 1941 In a signal timed 1635 C/8, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); An Italian warship was 60 miles north of Sirte at 2000 C/7. Benghazi air reconnaissance on the 7th reported one 10000 tons ship moored north and south in outer harbour, one 4000 tons ship along outer breakwater halfway between the break in sea wall and end. Two 1000 tons entering. (35)

9 May 1941 At 0620 C/9, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived for aircraft flying to the west of Benghazi. She then closed the harbour submerged to reconnoitre it, intending to remain off Benghazi if suitable targets were sighted.

At 0855 C/9, two schooners, apparently minesweeping the south-west approach to Benghazi were seen. Later a motor drifter, which had been seen before, was seen leaving the harbour. She streamed a sweep and passed 500 yards from HMS Triumph.

At 0930 C/9, HMS Triumph was one mile from the harbour. The only ships that could be seen were the wrecks and possibly a 1000-ton ship in the inner harbour, the this may also have been a wreck. Lt.Cdr. Woods decided to leave the area so as to reach Appolonia at daylight/10 as had been suggested by the Capt. S.1 in his signal timed 1555 C/5.
At 1000 C/9, one of the minesweeping schooners was seen to enter the harbour followed by a brigantine. The second schooner seen earlier was no longer in sight. (1)
10 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) bombards an enemy airport near Apollonia, Libya (near Susa). 48 Rounds were fired but the results could not be observed.
2045 C/10 - Surfaced and bombard aircraft hangars at the western end of the town. Fired 48 rounds from 2500 yards but the results could not be observed although several explosions were seen.
2058 C/10 - Ceased fire. Withdrew to seaward on the surface.
2115 C/10 - Set course for Alexandria. (1)
12 May 1941 Around 0920 C/12, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 14th war patrol at Alexandria. (1)
17 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is docked in the Gabbari graving dock at Alexandria. (37)
20 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is undocked. (37)
23 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Alexandria. (37)
25 May 1941 Around 2000 C/25, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Alexandria for her 15th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Sirte.
En-route to the patrol area she was to proceed through positions 31°46'N, 29°04'E and 33°03'N, 25°00'E. She was to arrive off Benghazi P.M./29.

30 May 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian armed merchant cruiser Ramb III (3667 GRT, built 1938) at Bengasi, Libya. She sank in shallow water and was later salvaged.
0524 C/30 - Dived 12 miles south-west [the patrol report says south-east but this is impossible] of Ras Tajunes and commenced to close the land to patrol northwards along the 20 fathom line.
Lt.Cdr. Woods decided to close Benghazi towards sunset to examine the harbour.

1818 C/30 - HMS Triumph was now 2 miles west of the harbour entrance. By this time four merchant vessels and several smaller craft had been identified in the harbour. The two largest of these, a 5000-ton cargo steamer and a Ramb-class motor ship were moored in the outer harbour with their sterns to the centre mole, unloading into lighters. Both were Folbot targets.
1820 C/30 - Went deeper to evade a patrolling aircraft.

1845 C/30 - Returned to periscope depth. Turned and closed the searched channel. While doing so it was noticed that both ships presented a clear target through the harbour entrance but that the 5000 tons ship was very high in the water and must have been (nearly) empty. It was decided to attack the Ramb-class vessel with a torpedo.

2022 C/30 - Fired two torpedoes (one was intended) and obtained one hit from a range of 3700 yards. The Ramb-class ship was shortly afterwards to settle by the bow and after a few minutes her forecastle was awash. Triumph withdrew to the northward.
2220 C/30 - Surfaced and shaped course for Misurata. (1)

2 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1521 C/2, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Keep to the west of 17°00'E from noon/5 at which time HMS Taku (Lt.Cdr. E.C.F. Nicolay, RN) will enter the patrol area east of 17°00'E and south of 32°45'N to operate off Benghazi. (38)

3 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1515 C/3, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Leave patrol P.M./5 to arrive in the Alexandria Sanctuary at 0500 C/12. [The route was included in the signal but is not given in the war diary summaries.] (38)

4 Jun 1941 At 2345 C/4, to the west of Sirte, Libya, in approximate position 31°20'N, 16°10'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a darkened object close inshore. The bearing altered very quickly to the eastward and the object was soon out of sight. It is considered possible this was a destroyer or torpedo boat. (1)

5 Jun 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary patrol vessels Valoroso (340 GRT), V 190 / Frieda (246 GRT) and V 137 / Trio Frassinetti (244 GRT) with gunfire in the Gulf of Sirte off Beurat, Libya in position 31°39'N, 15°39'E.

Valoroso had two killed and thirty-four survivors including five wounded. V 190 / Frieda had one killed and seven survivors. V 137 / Trio Frassinetti had no casualties but there were eight survivors. The survivors were picked up by the auxiliary gunboat Scilla and a seaplane or reached the coast in their lifeboats. A third schooner V 112 / Unione escaped to Buerat.
1313 C/5 - Spotted three schooners (estimated at 300 tons each) escorted by an A/S trawler coming up astern.
1440 C/5 - Surfaced and engaged the A/S trawler with gunfire from 600 yards. The trawler was on fire after 24 rounds. Two of the schooners were sunk with 9 and 7 rounds respectively.
The third schooner, which was about a mile astern of the other two managed to escape to shallow water.

Lt.Cdr. Woods then returned to the trawler that was showing no signs of sinking, therefore another round was fired that holed the ship on the waterline. Triumph now retreated seawards. The trawler was seen to sink an hour later.
1515 C/5 - Withdrew to seaward, to get into deeper water, on the surface at 15 knots.
1550 C/5 - Dived and continued to withdraw to seaward. (1)

6 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 0745 C/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Estimate Triumph to be position 33°45'N, 22°10'E at 0500 C/9. From that position proceed direct to reconnoitre Gavdo Island where there is reported to be an unknown number of British military personnel. Endeavour to verify this but do not spend more than 24 hours on this reconnaissance. Embark as many as practicable and on completion proceed direct to Alexandria, signal any information obtained as quickly as possible.
The signal was only deciphered at 1030 C/7 and Lt.Cdr. Woods decided to make the passage there on the surface to be there as fast as possible. (1)

7 Jun 1941 At 2300 C/7, in position 33°29'N, 19°07'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted the lights of a ship bearing 180° at a range of 6 miles. HMS Triumph closed to investigate and the vessel was seen to be the Italian hospital ship Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) proceeding on course 330° at 13 knots. She was on the Benghazi - Messina route.
At 2330 C/7, the hospital ship passed at a range of 3000 yards. HMS Triumph resumed her passage.
The Arno had sailed from Benghazi at 1530 B/7 with 420 wounded, including 56 Germans, and arrived at Naples at 0845 B/9. (1)
8 Jun 1941 Around 1900 C/8, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived off Gavdos Island. A suitable place to land a Folbot party was now sought.
2240 C/8 - Started to close the land.
2330 C/8 - Launched the Folbot Folbot manned by S.Lt. Don (from the crew of HMS Triumph) and Corporal Bremner (see 9 June for the continuation of the events). (1)
9 Jun 1941(continuation of the evens of 8 June 1941) 0230 C/9 - The Folbot returned to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN). They had not seen any British soldiers.
0700 C/9 - Closed the land with a large British flag flying from the periscope. A few people appeared on the beach including what looked like four unarmed Greek soldiers.

0730 C/9 - Launched the Folbot to bring back one of the Greeks. A civilian was brought back who said in French that there were no British soldiers left on the island and that there were a few German soldiers at the Southern end. He urged us to clear the area before German aircraft might arrive. They also said that about 18 captured British soldiers were evacuated to Athens by German aircraft. Triumph now left the area and set course towards Alexandria. (1)
12 Jun 1941 Around 0730 C/12, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 15th war patrol at Alexandria. (1)
22 Jun 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) conducted sound trials at Alexandria. (39)
26 Jun 1941 Around 1015 C/26, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Alexandria for her 16th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Sirte.
Before proceeding on patrol attack exercises were carried out during which the Greek torpedo boat RHS Aspis acted as target. (40)
26 Jun 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Triumph during her 16th war patrol see the map below.

27 Jun 1941 The Italian submarine Salpa was torpedoed and sunk near Mersa Matruh, Egypt in position 32°05'N, 28°47'E by the British submarine HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN). There were no survivors, her commanding officer T.V. (Lt.) Renato Guagni, 4 officers and 44 ratings were lost.

0800 C/27 - A surfaced submarine was sighted about 2000 yards away. As the enemy manoeuvring made it impossible to make a submerged torpedo attack Triumph surfaced at 0811 hours and opened fire with the deck and Lewis guns from 1500 yards.

The enemy also manned his deck gun. After firing 33 rounds (with 5 hits) the enemy was almost stopped and appeared to be sinking by the stern. The enemy's gun crew, after firing five rounds that all went over, abandoned their gun and went below.

0818 C/27 - Fired two torpedoes from 600 yards. The second torpedo hit the enemy submarine. An enormous column of smoke and water shot into the air and debris showered all round. A large pool of oil appeared but no survivors were seen in the water.

2142 C/27 - Passed our signal timed 1937 C/27 to the Capt. S.1 which contained the following; One submarine sunk by gunfire and two torpedoes at 0818 C/27. Position, course and speed of the enemy were 32°05'N, 26°47'E, 003° at 10 knots. (41)

28 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1351 C/28, the Capt. S.1 sent the following to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Your 1931 C/27, Well done. Cancel previous orders and proceed with all despatch towards Cape Matapan. HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) was ordered to proceed towards Cape Matapan to be in a position to intercept an expected Vichy-French convoy with reinforcements for Syria.

In a signal timed 1521 C/28, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Triumph, HMS Perseus (Lt.Cdr. P.J.H. Bartlett, RN) and HMS Torbay (Lt.Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, RN) to form a patrol line 090° from Cape Malea at 0400 C/30. (42)
