30 Jun 1941 At 0448 C/30, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived in estimated position 093° - Cape Malea - 5 miles. She then closed the land to check her position by bearings

At 0618 C/30, a large caique was seen steering 240° towards Kithera. HMS Triumph closed to investigate. The caique was seen to have a deck cargo, some of which were seen to be barrels. This caique could have been sunk as she was thought to be either flying an Italian or German flag. Lt.Cdr. Woods however decided not to reveal the submarines presence jeopardising the patrol line.
At 1516 C/30, two more caiques were seen but likewise not attacked. (43)

1 Jul 1941 At 1920 C/1, when in approximate position 36°30'N, 23°17'E, HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a caique bearing 210°, steering a course of 360°. It was not attacked so as not to reveal the presence of the submarine. (44)

1 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1755 C/1, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN), HMS Torbay (Lt.Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, RN) and HMS Perseus (Lt.Cdr. P.J.H. Bartlett, RN); Cancel my signal timed 1521 C/28. Assume patrol as follows; Perseus off Cape Malea as in my signal timed 1455 C/24. Triumph in the Gulf of Sirte as ordered in your patrol orders, routed through Kithera Channel and position 32°50'N, 20°00'E. Torbay through the Zea Channel to area 'E'. (37°32'N, 24°00'E - 000° - 65 - 95). (45)
3 Jul 1941 At 0430 C/3, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2129 C/2 in which her patrol area was extended to the west to 15°00'E. (42)

5 Jul 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sinks, during a gun battle, the Italian merchant vessel Ninfea (607 GRT, built 1917) and the Italian tug Dante de Lutti (266 GRT) off Ras Tajunes, Libya. During this gun battle HMS Triumph sustained some damage.
The Dante de Lutti is sunk and the Ninfea partially sunk but was apparently a total loss. One officer was killed and four ratings were wounded.
1539 C/5 - Sighted a ship close inshore near Ras Tajunes. Closed to investigate.

The target was eventually identified as a 1500 ton steamship, very light forward. The enemy was anchored close to the two fathom spit running south from Ras Tajunes. She appeared to be aground until it was seen that she was yawing heavily. There was too much swell to fire a torpedo set to run shallow. Cdr. Woods decied to attack with the gun after dark.
2024 C/5 - Sighted a trawler approaching from the northward. This trawler turned north again and was soon list in the failing light.
2156 C/5 - Surfaced and closed the target.
2222 C/5 - Sighted the target and another small ship near her. This was eventually seen to be an armed trawler at anchor, possibly the one seen at 2024 C/5.

2244 C/5 - Triumph was now only 100 yards from the trawler when fire was opened with the deck gun and a Lewis gun. After 6 rounds of flash less 4", all of which hit, the trawler was on fire, steam was escaping from the boiler and the ready use ammunition for it's gun had exploded. A round was fired into the trawlers waterline. Fire was now shifted to the merchant.

Fire on the merchant was opened at 800 yards. After 15 rounds, with several hits, the stock of flash less ammunition was gone and regular shells had to be used. After the first one was fired Triumph was fired upon by a shore battery. A round from the shore battery fell close enough to wet the bridge and gun crew. Actually it was a hit forward but this was not realised at that time. Triumph withdrew from the scene. Later it was found out that no.1 ballast tank had been holed on the waterline. (42)
6 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1215 C/6, the Capt. S.1's sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Remain on patrol until 12th and arrive at Alexandria on the 17th. (45)

9 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1429 C/9, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt.Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Leave patrol on 12 July to arrive in the Alexandria Sanctuary at 0451 C/17 [route included in the signal but not in the war diary summaries. HMS Taku (Lt.Cdr. E.C.F. Nicolay, RN) will be off Apollonia noon/12 proceed thence 32°30'N, 20°00'E. (45)
10 Jul 1941 At 1600 A/10, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) received the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1258 B/10 which contained the following order; Proceed to Malta now. (45)
12 Jul 1941 Around 0900 B/12, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 16th war patrol at Malta. (42)
19 Jul 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is docked at the Malta Dockyard in No.1 graving dock for a short refit.
[As there is no log available for August 1941 it is not known to us when she was undocked].

19 Aug 1941 Around 1700 C/19, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Malta for her 17th war patrol. She was ordered to carry out a special operation which has the object of destroying a railway bridge on the north coast of Sicily near Torrente Furiano about 55 miles east of Palermo.

On board were Lt. D. Schofield, Royal Fusiliers and eleven other ranks of No.2 Commando with eight Folbots, 2015 lbs of TNT plastic explosives and other demolition stores. The reload torpedoes had been landed to provide stowage for these stores.
[No log is available for August 1941 so map can be displayed.] (42)
20 Aug 1941 At 0414 C/20, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived in position 37°24'N, 12°42'E to make the passage through Q.B.B. 65 [mined area] at 150 feet at a speed of 3 knots.
Between 1442 C/20 and 1524 C/20 - In approximate position 37°40'N, 12°17'E, heard eight fairly loud explosions at about five minute intervals.
At 1900 C/20, HMS Triumph came to periscope depth and fixed the position as being 193° - Marettimo light - 9 miles.
At 2125 C/20, HMS Triumph surfaced. (42)
21 Aug 1941 At 0544 C/21, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived in about 13 miles to the south of Ustica. Course was set for Alicudi Island at periscope depth at a speed of 3 knots.
At 2155 C/21, the lights of a ship were sighted which was proceeding eastwards along the Sicilian coast. This was probably a hospital ship.
[We have been unable to find any hospital ship movement in this area on that day.] (42)

21 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1415 B/21, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) that a convoy of three Vichy-French ships would pass through position 41°35'N, 10°00'E about 0001/21 and would then proceed towards the Messina Strait. They were expected to pass through position 37°03'N, 16°46'E around 2000 B/22. They were not to be attacked.

This referred to the Vichy-French evacuation convoy 'C' which was made up of the liners Marrakech (6288 GRT, built 1914), Sinaia (8567 GRT, built 1922) and Groix (9957 GRT, built 1922). This convoy had departed Toulon on 19 August and arrived at Beirut on 28 August. At Beirut Marrakech embarked 733 passengers, Sinaia embarked 1867 passengers and Groix embarked 1562 passengers. Total number of passengers was 4162 of which 3674 were military personnel. The convoy departed Beirut on 29 August and arrived at Marseille on 5 September. (46)
22 Aug 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived off Torrente Furiano and carried out periscope reconnaissance during the forenoon and shortly before sunset. (42)

23 Aug 1941 Shortly after midnight, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) closed the coast to land a reconnaissance party but did not do so due to considerable groundswell and surf. Triumph then withdrew to the seaward.
0530 C/23 - Dived and closed the beach to check position and set.
1745 C/23 - Retired to seaward. During the day the swell had died down and conditions now seemed favourable to go ahead with the operation.

During the evening Triumph received a signal (Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1925 B/23) that the enemy battle fleet was at sea and that she was to proceed to a position to cover the north approaches to Messina upon completion of her special operation tonight.
The signal ordered HMS Triumph to patrol in position 38°22'N, 15°35'E (north-west of the Straits of Messina) on completion of tonight's operation.
Cdr. Woods decided to go ahead with the reconnaissance this night and postpone the actual operation until the return journey.
The enemy battleships referred to were the Littorio and Vittorio Veneto escorted by the destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere, Alpino, Aviere and Camicia Nera.

They had sailed from Taranto at 1450 B/22-1610 B/22 in anticipation of an enemy squadron which sailed from Gibraltar and crossed the Straits of Messina at 0730 B/23-0800 B/23. They operated in the Tyrrhenian Sea. At 1635 B/23, they were reinforced by the destroyers Nicoloso Da Recco, Lanzerotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi. They had sailed from Naples at 1232 B/23-1405 B/23 (so did he destroyers Freccia, Folgore and Fulmine which sailed at 2030 B/23-2045 B/23 and then went to Palermo, arriving at 1000 B/25-1110 B/25). At 1845 B/23, they sighted the cruisers of the Third Cruiser Division (see below). During the early hours of the 24th, the minelayer HMS Manxman (Capt. R.K. Dickson, RN) successfully laid a minefield between Leghorn and Piombino but was not detected. At 2035 B/24-2216 B/24, the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Alpino, Fuciliere, Granatiere, Bersagliere and Maestrale reached Cagliari to refuel and sailed again at 0350 B/25-0530 B/25. The two battleships, screened by Camicia Nera, Antoniotto Usodimare, Alpino, Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio Da Noli, Nicoloso Da Recco and ?, reached Naples at 0647 B/26-0834 B/26. Aviere reached Naples at 1230 B/26. Emanuele Pessagno arrived at Naples at 1617 B/26.

Other movements: The Third Cruiser Division departed from Messina at 0955 B/23-1010 B/23: heavy cruisers Trieste, Trento, Bolzano and Gorizia, screened by the destroyers Carabiniere and Corazziere. Ascari and Lanciere, sailed from Reggio (Calabria) at 0900 B/23-0940 B/23 to join them. We know they joined the Battle Squadron at 1845 B/23 but it is not clear when they were detached. They returned to Messina at 0728 B/26-0815 B/26, with the following exceptions: as they approached their destination, Bolzano was torpedoed by HMS Triumph at 0638 B/26 and reached Messina at 1306 B/26. Corazziere entered Messina at 1130 B/26 and Carabiniere at 1220 B/26, Ascari and Lanciere returned to Reggio at 1228 B/26-1259 B/26. The destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Emanuele Pessagno, Giuseppe Da Verazzano, Antoniotto Usodimare and Antonio Da Noli sailed from Trapani at 1915-2020 B/23. The destroyers Maestrale and Scirocco sailed from Palermo at 1240 B/23-1330 B/23. Maestrale and Scirocco returned to Palermo at 0130 B/26 and 1831 B/26 respectively. Da Verazzano, Pigafetta and Malocelo arrived at La Spezia at 1400 B/26, 1345 B/26 and 1403 B/26 respectively.

The Eighth Cruiser Division, made up of the light cruisers Luigi Di Savoia Duca Degli Abruzzi, Muzio Attendolo and Raimondo Montecuccoli, screened by five destroyers, including the Fulmine, departed from Palermo at 0530 B/24-0630 B/24. They returned to Palermo at 0830 B/24-0940 B/25.

The Italian squadrons reported six submarine sightings: 1. 1039 B/24 by the destroyer Fulmine (318° - Marettimo – 30 miles) sighted two torpedo tracks and dropped depth charges (this was HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN)). 2. 1745 B/24 by the battleship Vittorio Veneto (off Cape Spartivento, Sardinia). 3. 1219 B/25 by the destroyer Nicoloso Da Recco (off Cape Spartivento, Sardinia). 4. 1238 B/25 by the destroyer Aviere (48 miles SSE of Cape Spartivento, Sardinia). 5. 1845 B/25 by the destroyer Granatiere and the battleship Littorio (85 miles east of Cape Carbonara, Sardinia). 6. 1900 B/25 by the battleship Vittorio Veneto (off Cape Carbonara, Sardinia). The last five sighting seems all to be bogus. (42)

24 Aug 1941 At 0130 C/24, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) landed her reconnaissance party (Lt. Schofield, two Sergeants and a Petty Officer) in two folbots. They returned one hour later. No opposition was encountered. Triumph now set course for her ordered patrol position.
At 1830 C/24, HMS Triumph arrived in the ordered patrol position, 38°22'N, 15°35'E.
At 2130 C/24, HMS Triumph surfaced and withdrew to the northward to charge.
At 2205 C/24, a motor torpedo boat was seen at a range of 1000 yards. HMS Triumph dived to 80 feet surfacing again at 2340 C/24. (42)
25 Aug 1941 At 0001 C/25, to the north-west of the Straits of Messina HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a destroyer approaching for which she dived to 80 feet to evade.
At 0138 C/25, HMS Triumph surfaced. HE persisted for an hour after diving but surfacing was delayed to make good a defect in the port main engine.
At 0224 C/25, a destroyer was seen crossing ahead at a range of 3000 yards. HMS Triumph turned away but remained on the surface.
At 0518 C/25, HMS Triumph dived three miles to the north of her patrol position and patrolled at periscope depth in the vicinity of the patrol position.
At 0720 C/25, a laden two-masted schooner under sail was seen proceeding westwards along the Sicilian coast.

At 0825 C/25, a destroyer was sighted approaching from the north-westward. This vessel was subsequently identified as the submarine chaser Albatros. It was considered that this ship had also been encountered at 0001 C/25 and 0224 C/25. She continued towards Messina at 16 knots.
Most likely this was indeed the Albatros which had sailed from Messina on 24 August (time unknown) and returned there at 1130 B/25.
At 1020 C/25, two motor trawlers were seen approaching steering 280°. HMS Triumph took avoiding action. These trawlers were minesweepers and were seen to return still sweeping three hours later.
At 1110 C/25, a motor drifter was seen approaching from the north-eastward. This vessel remained about 3 to 4 miles away until around 1600 C/25.
At 2130 C/25, HMS Triumph withdrew to the westward about 10 to 15 miles. No patrol were encountered during the night. (42)
26 Aug 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian heavy cruiser Bolzano north of Messina, Sicily, Italy.

According to Italian sources she was hit astern, lost her screws and rudder but reached Messina. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori and the submarine chaser Albatros were sent to hunt the submarine. Missori lost her towing A/S mine and assumed it had caught the submarine, erroneously claiming a sinking but HMS Triumph was far away.
0513 C/26- Dived 2 miles bearing 57° from the patrol position.
0554 C/26 - Heard fairly loud depth charging getting nearer.

0600 C/26 - In position 38°22'N, 15°38'E sighted a number of ships to the North-Westward. The light was still very poor, and as the high power periscope was out of action it was some minutes before these were seen to be three battleships or cruisers escorted by about ten destroyers.
0611 C/26 - Started attack.

0638 C/26 - Fired two torpedoes from 5300 yards at the rear cruiser. Immediately upon firing Triumph went to 80 feet and cleared the firing position by setting course to the Northward. One muffled explosion was heard approximately three minutes after firing. A second muffled explosion was heard eleven minutes after firing.

0647 C/26 - Returned to periscope depth. The visibility in the direction of Messina was still very poor, but as far as could be seen only two large ships were entering the Straits. Some way astern of them hardly visible against the land, was what appeared to be a stationary ship with a column of smoke rising from it. Several minutes later aircraft and destroyers appeared and started dropping depth charges. It seemed very likely that one of the torpedoes did hit the rear cruiser. Due to the very heavy A/S activity Cdr. Woods retired to seaward.

0740 C/26 - Sighted what appeared to be the masts of a trawler to the southward in which direction there was a considerable mirage. A second pair of masts appeared and soon resolved themselves into what was thought to be a pair of destroyers. HMS Triumph continued to retired to the north.

0805 C/26 - It was now seen that this 'pair of destroyers' were in fact two large unescorted merchant vessels,with a third some way astern. They were approaching the Straits of Messina from the south of Vulcano Island. There was by now no chance of getting within range and the three merchant vessels subsequently entered the Straits of Messina by the same route as had the Italian Fleet had done. The ships were not definitely identified but all were at least 10000 tons. One had three funnels and the remainder two and they appeared to be painted in peace time colours. This and the fact that they were unescorted made Cdr. Woods suspect that they were Vichy-French, although no information had been received of an eastbound Vichy-French convoy.' [In a signal timed 1117 B/24, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta had informed HMS Triumph of this convoy but perhaps the signal had not been received.]

This was the Vichy French ' Convoy D ' which were on round trip to Syria. The convoy was made up of the André Lebon (14365 GRT, built 1915), Colombie (13391 GRT, built 1931) and Massilia (passenger / cargo (reefer) ship, 15147 GRT, built 1920). They sailed from Toulon on 23 August and arrived in Beirut on 29 August. At Beirut the André Lebon embarked 1785 passengers, Colombie embarked 1376 passengers and Massilia embarked 2547 passengers. A total of 5700 were embarked in these three ships of which 4235 were military personnel. They departed Beirut on 1 September and arrived at Marseille on 8 September.

1720 C/26 - Received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1347 B/26 which contained the following orders; Leave patrol at 2030 B/26 and proceed through position 38°20'N, 12°40'E thence direct to position 'J' to make passage to position 'H' during daylight/28 to arrive at Malta before 2030 B/29 or at 065OB/3O.

2206 C/26 - Passed an enemy report and the intention to complete the special operation on the night of 27/28 August to Malta W/T. This was HMS Triumph's signal timed 1913 B/26. Set course for a position to the west of Alicudi Island, passing to the north of Stromboli. (42)

26 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1003 B/26, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) of the following; Reported motor torpedo boat maintaining patrol between 38°26'N and Messina, east of 15°20'E. (46)
27 Aug 1941 During the day, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived back off Torrente Furiano. Shortly before dusk periscope reconnaissance was carried out.

Shortly before midnight all canoes, gear and personnel were on the upper casing when a boat was sighted that was thought to be a patrol vessel [according to Italian sources this was the small fishing vessel No.231/2]. It was tried to ram the vessel but it was missed by inches. It was now fired upon with the Lewis guns. It was however soon seen that the boat was a fishing vessel. To avoid further compromise the fisherman were captured (several of them were wounded) and their boat was sunk.
Cdr. Woods decided to postpone the operation for 48 hours and retired from the area.
In a signal timed 2018 B/28, which was passed at 0002 C/29, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta was informed of the delay. (42)

29 Aug 1941 During the day, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) arrived back off Torrente Furiano. Shortly before dusk periscope reconnaissance was carried out. She withdrew to seaward upon completion. (42)

30 Aug 1941 Between 0049 C/30-0113 C/30, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) launched 9 canoes (with 13 saboteurs led by Lt. Schofield) off Torrente Furiano to destroy the railway bridge there. (the was 1 single canoe and 4 towing canoes 4 others with demolitions and stores).
0115 C/30 - Triumph withdrew slowly to seaward to charge. It was intended to return to within one nautical mile of the beach at 0330 hours.
0320 C/30 - Saw one explosion from the direction of the bridge.

0340 C/30 - Stopped one mile of the shore and drifted in with the tide to half a mile. It was not before 0507 hours before one of the canoes and it's tow was identified to the seaward of Triumph. They were quickly picked up. A second canoe and tow was spotted at 0527 hours. No more canoes were sighted after this time. Triumph had to clear the area as it was getting light. The railway bridge was seen to be partially destroyed. The remainder of the raiding party could not be picked up. Eight saboteurs including Lt. Schofield were captured by the Italians.
Triumph returned to the area the next night but no one turned up at the rendezvous and at 0430 C/31, Cdr. Woods decided to commence the return passage to Malta. (42)

30 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1013 B/30, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) of the following; Six merchant ships and five destroyers, speed 9 knots, off Kerkennah steering northward at 0900 B/30.

This was the convoy made up of the Italian transports Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1926), Marin Sanudo (5958 GRT, built 1926), Nicolo Odero (6003 GRT, built 1925) and the Italian tanker Minatitlán (7651 GRT, built 1941). The Italian auxiliary magnetic minesweeper Eritrea (2517 GRT, built 1918) was also part of this convoy which was escorted by the Italian destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Euro and the Italian torpedo boats Calliope, Orsa and Pegaso. This convoy sailed from Tripoli at 1745 B29 - 1835 B/29 and reached Naples at 1240 B - 1435 B/1. (46)
2 Sep 1941 Around 0825 C/2, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 17th war patrol at Malta. (42)
6 Sep 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (47)
9 Sep 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is undocked. (47)
16 Sep 1941 Around 1815 B/16, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Malta for her 18th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Adriatic and to perform a special operation.
On board were two Army officers, three Yugoslav air force officers and two Corporals of No.2 Commando.
18 Sep 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian tanker Ardor (8960 GRT, built 1927) off Capo Colonna.
Ardor was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Orione.

Ardor, escorted by Orione, sailed from Reggio Calabria at 1655 B/17 for Taranto. After the torpedoing, Orione dropped 10 100-kg and 49 50-kg depth charges, Ardor managed to proceed under her own power and stopped off the entrance of Crotone at 1120 B/18 and finally entered harbour at 1400 B/18.
0550 B/18 - Dived a little over two miles to the east of Cape Rizzuto and patrolled northwards at periscope depth.
0813 B/18 - Sighted the 6000 tons Italian tanker Liri (this was thus incorrect) escorted to seaward by a Spica-class torpedo boat coming up from astern. Started attack from the inshore side.
0843 B/18 - Fired three torpedoes from 2500 yards. One hit was obtained. Upon firing Triumph cleared the area at high submerged speed.

0848 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth. The tanker could be seen heading inshore with a heavy list to port and well down by the stern. The torpedo boat was steaming up and down her seaward side, dropping depth charges. The tanker appeared to be heading towards the beach. Cdr. Woods decided to remain to the seaward until the torpedo boat dropped its last depth charge and then try to turn inshore again to finish off the tanker.

0950 B/18 - After 44 depth charges no more were dropped and the torpedo boat was seen to make off to the North. Meanwhile the tanker could no longer be seen and it was hoped that she had sunk. Unfortunately shortly afterwards she was seen rounding Capo Crotone out of range for another attack.
1045 B/18 - Passed between two motor torpedo boats which were sweeping southwards.

1520 B/18 - A small brigantine was seen to leave Crotone harbour and she subsequently joined two others which were anchored to the south of the town. A pilot or examination vessel was lying off the southern harbour.
1720 B/18 - Withdrew to seaward.
1740 B/18 - Sighted the two motor torpedo boats returning to harbour. (42)
20 Sep 1941 In the evening (after dark), HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) landed a Yugoslav party of five men off Petrovac, Macedonia.
0117 B/20 - Sighted the lights of a hospital ship which eventually passed on course 090° at 7 knots in position 41°12'N, 18°37'E. This was on the Bari / Durazzo route.
The hospital ship sighted was the Italian Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) which sailed from Bari at 1800 B/18 and arrived at Durazzo at 0720 B/20.
1400 B/20 - Arrived off Petrovac. Some difficulty was experienced in recognising landmarks.

2100 B/20 - It was decided to postpone charging until after the landing to give the party ample time to get clear by daylight. Surfaced half a mile offshore and launched the first canoe containing Capt. Hudson, S.Lt. Don and one Yugoslav officer. The wind made the handling of the canoe difficult and it took an hour before this canoe returned to report that the landing place was ideal. A second canoe was then launched to complete the landing. Three more trips from each of the canoes being required. As soon as it was evident that there were no sentries ashore HMS Triumph closed to 300 yards from the beach, where the submarine was hidden by cliffs except from the beach and from seaward. She was then also in sheltered water. (42)

20 Sep 1941 In a signal timed 1616 B/20, the Capt. S.1 sent the following info / orders to HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Probably little traffic in Bauxite but timber and lignite being shipped from Sibenik and Dubrovnik, routes not known. Everything fair game no questions need be asked. (48)

21 Sep 1941 At 1504 B/21, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted a ship approaching along the coast from the direction of Cavtat. The submarine then closed the channel at the eastern side of Kalocep Island at speed, but it was not possible to get within attacking range. The target was a tramp-type vessel of about 1500 ton. The enemy rounded Daksa Island and then made for Gruz.

This was possibly the steamer Carlo Zeno (Italian, 1446 GRT, built 1906) which had sailed from Durazzo (Durrës) at 1000 B/20 at 7 knots for Ragusa, where her ETA was 1430 B/21 (she may have arrived an hour or so later).

1615 B/21 - Sighted an empty three-masted auxiliary schooner approaching along the coast from Cavtat. Three similar vessels, all empty, a 400 ton coastal passenger steamer, all flying Italian colours, also appeared from the direction of Cavtat during the next hour. None were worth torpedoing and Cdr. Woods did not consider it worth giving away the submarines presence by gunning them.

The 400-ton coastal steamer may have been the Corriere del Tevere (Italian, 423 GRT, built 1879) which sailed from Durazzo at 0430 B/20 at 8 knots for Metcovich where her ETA was 1800 B/21 (she may have arrived later).

At 1745 B/21, HMS Triumph sighted a merchant vessel of about 2500 tons off Daksa Island. The submarine then closed the channel east of Kalocep Island but by the time Cdr. Woods had a clear view the merchant vessel had made off towards Gruz.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel. A remote possibility is the Città di Catania (3355 GRT, built 1910) which may have been in the area.]
At 1848 B/21 - HMS Triumph withdrew to seaward surfacing at 2045 B/21 5 miles to the south of Sveti Andrija at 2045 A/21. Course was set to be off Vis at dawn the following morning. (42)

22 Sep 1941 At 0535 B/22, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived 15 miles to the south-west of Vis. She then commenced to patrol northwards and eastwards leaving Vis to starboard. At 1318 B/22, when in approximate position 43°06'N, 15°57'E, a ship was sighted leaving Kamen [Komiza ???] Cove. HMS Triumph closed but could not get within range. The target proved to be a small coastal passenger steamer doing about 12 knots. This ship was not worth torpedoing. She went into Vis and later to Hvar.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel, most likely it was a local ferry.]
At 2045 B/22, HMS Triumph surfaced and patrolled to the north-west of Vis during the night. (42)
23 Sep 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) torpedoed and sank the German merchant vessel Luvsee (2373 GRT, built 1898) north-east of Sibenek.

The Luvsee was carrying bauxite. Only 4 of the crew of 20 were rescued by the Italian patrol vessel B 449 / N. Sauro. She also picked up 3 bodies (one of these was of Captain Albert Schmidt). The German ship had sailed from Sibenik and was bound for Trieste.
0531 B/23 - Dived 6 miles south of the Mulo Island lighthouse. Closed the coast to 1.5 miles and patrolled to the south-eastwards.
0804 B/23 - When in position 43°29'N, 15°55'E sighted an unescorted 3500-tons merchant vessel flying the Italian flag approaching from Sibenik. Started attack.

0842 B/23 - Near position 43°29'N, 15°55'E fired four torpedoes from 3600 yards. One hit under the targets bridge was obtained. The stern of the target was seen to rise high in the air and the ship sank in less than a minute in position 43°31'N, 15°50'E.
Between 0915 B/23 and 1053 B/23 - Reloaded at 80 feet. On completion commenced to patrol to the south-east intending to close the land if no A/S activity occurred.

1309 B/23 - Sighted a destroyer, later identified as a former Yugoslavian Beograd-class, steering north-west alond the coast. The enemy turned south-west off Mulo Island and stopped near the position in which the merchant vessel had been sunk which was then about 6 miles from the submarine. The destroyer returned to harbour after about half an hour at 20 knots. Presumably she had picked up the survivors.

The only Beograd-class destroyer in commission in the Italian navy on that day was the Sebenico but she was in Naples on that day so it could not be this vessel. The other Beograd-class destroyer, the Lubiana had not yet been commissioned by the Italian Navy and was still undergoing repairs at Fiume.
Most probably the vessel seen was the Italian torpedo boat T 6 (former Yugoslavian) which sailed from Spalato (Split) at 1115 B/23 to hunt the submarine and returned at 1540 B/23.

1708 B/23 - Closed the land and patrolled to the north-west. Several small auxiliary sailing vessels of about 50 tons were seen. These were not attacked with the gun due the proximity of a fort in which three guns could be seen. Also it was hoped that something bigger might arrive before dark.
2043 B/23 - Surfaced. Cdr. Woods had meanwhile decided to leave the area following the sinking and to patrol off Ortona. (42)
24 Sep 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) damaged the Italian water tanker Poseidone (6533 GRT, built 1912). The Italian vessel had sailed from Bari at 1500 B/23 at 7.5 knots for Venice.

The crew of the Poseidone had two members wounded. It was the freighter Sidamo (Italian, 2263 GRT, built 1895), which was undergoing repairs in Ortona, which had opened fire on the submarine, forcing her to dive and not shore a battery. The tug that took Poseidone in tow was the Loredan.
0537 B/24 - Dived 9 miles, 035° from Ortona.
1207 B/24 - Closed to 5 miles from the coast and commenced to patrol to the south-east.
1221 B/24 - Sighted an unescorted tanker of about 6000-tons, 20° on the starboard bow at a range of 9000 yards. Started attack.
1243 B/24 - Near position 42°24'N, 14°31'E fired four torpedoes from 2500 yards. One torpedo hit just abaft the bridge. The tanker's speed was reduced to 3 knots.

1253 B/24 - Fired one torpedo from 1600 yards to finish her off. It hit abreast the funnel. It caused the target to stop and list heavily to starboard but it did not sink. It was intended to fire another torpedo but Cdr. Woods was unable to do so as the sea was covered in oil and it's time the periscope was raised in the oil covered water the oil would stick to the periscope and he could not see anything.
1336 B/24 - After increasing the range to get out of the oil fired a torpedo from 3500 yards. It missed.
1344 B/24 - Fired Triumph's last torpedo from 3000 yards but this one also missed.

1346 B/24 - Cdr. Woods took Triumph to seaward to deeper water. He was hoping to see the tanker settle in the water but it did not do so. Meanwhile three small craft had appeared on the scene and he feared that it were A/S vessels but they soon turned out to be a tug and two small motor vessels like pilot cutters.
1418 B/24 - The tug had now taken the tanker in tow so Cdr. Woods decided to engage with gunfire.
1432 B/24 - Surfaced and opened fire on the tug, which quickly slipped her tow. After 6 rounds fire was shifted to the tanker.

1434 B/24 - A shore battery opened a slow and inaccurate gunfire. Meanwhile several hits had been obtained on the tanker. One of the pilot cutters also took a hit and was not seen again and was most likely sunk.

1442 B/24 - The shore battery now began to find the range so the action was broken off and Triumph submerged. As Cdr. Woods closed the conning tower hatch a shell passed close overhead. Course was now set to the seaward to clear the area.
2040 B/24 - Surfaced and set course for Lagosta Island. (42)

26 Sep 1941 At 0525 B/26, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived in position 065° - Bari - 12 miles. She then proceeded to a position 3 miles to the east of the harbour of Bari to conduct a periscope reconnaissance to search for a suitable place for train wrecking but no suitable place was found.

HMS Triumph then proceeded to the north-west to reconnoitre the harbour of Bari. Five merchant vessels (four 3000 to 5000 tons merchant vessels and a small coaster) and two hospital ships (one two-funnelled, one with one funnel) were seen to be present in the harbour. No naval vessels were seen.
HMS Triumph then proceeded to the south-west along the coast until 1830 B/26.

During the day, one merchant vessel, two motor torpedo boats, all southbound and three merchant vessels and one schooner northbound were seen. All these merchant vessels were unescorted. The largest northbound merchant vessel, of about 6000 tons, had a deck cargo of motor buses.
Details of the ships sighted were as follows; 0820 B/26 - 1700 ton coaster, flying light, steering 115°.
0829 B/26 - Small schooner, course 115°, minesweeping.
1215 B/27 - Three similar vessels as the one seen at 0829 B/26 were seen leaving Bari. They came very near to HMS Triumph but eventually disappeared while sweeping on bearing 070° from Bari.
1300 B/26 - Three-masted schooner, course 295°. Seen to enter Bari.
1327 B/26 - 3500-ton merchant vessel. Flying light. Course 290°. Did not enter Bari.

1600 B/26 - 6000-ton motor ship. Flying light but with a deck cargo of motor buses. Course 290°. Was preparing to enter harbour, probably Bari. This vessel was armed with machine guns. Also a 300-ton coaster was seen, flying light. Course 290°. Two motor torpedo boats. Course 115°. Speed 35 knots.

The 6000-tons motor vessel may have been the steamer (not a motor vessel !) Polcevere (Italian, 2609 GRT, built 1920) which sailed from Brindisi at 0710 B/26 and arrived at Bari at ? (no mention in the Taranto Group Command but time could be a match).
1630 B/26 - The minesweeper seen at 0829 B/26 was seen returning. (42)

27 Sep 1941 At 0525 B/27, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted the lights of a hospital ship. This was almost certainly the same ship that had been seen on the 20th. It is thought she was en-route to Bari coming from Valona or Corfu.

This was the Italian Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) which arrived at Porto Edda at 0800 B/26 and sailed from there for Valona at 1030 B/26. She sailed from Valona at 1900 B/26 and arrived at Bari at 1200 B/27.?
At 0525 B/27, HMS Triumph dived in position 275° - Saseno Island - 11 miles.

At 0635 B/27, two fishing boats under sail and the masts and bowspirt of schooner were seen approaching about 6 miles astern. HMS Triumph subsequently went to 150 feet to make the southbound passage of the Otranto Straits.
At 2043 B/27, HMS Triumph surfaced in position 260° - Fano Island - 24 miles.

At 2251 B/27, HMS Triumph passed her signal timed 1852 B/27 which contained the following; No torpedoes but 126 rounds of ammunition remain. Intend diving position 38°28'N, 19°36'E at dawn/28, course 143°. Request route. Special operation completed during the night of the 20th. [Some details on the operations were also included in the signal]. Searched channel extends 070° from Bari. Seven ships in Bari harbour on the 26th. Did not attack in this area. (42)

28 Sep 1941 In a signal timed 0108 B/28, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN); Your 1852 B/27 acknowledged. Well done. Proceed through position 34°38'N, 21°44'E thence direct arriving in the Alexandria Submarine Sanctuary at 0530 B/3. (48)
3 Oct 1941 Around 0945 B/3, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 18th war patrol at Alexandria. (42)
12 Oct 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Alexandria. (49)

16 Oct 1941 Around 1015 B/16, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) departed from Alexandria for her 19th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean. She is also to perform two special operations.

On departure a practice attack was made during which HMS Falk (Lt. H.S. Upperton, RNR) acted as target. This attack was followed by A/S exercises with HMS Falk and HMS Kingston Coral (Ch.Skr. I. Mackintosh, MBE, RNR). (42)
16 Oct 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Triumph during her 19th war patrol see the map below.

18 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1551 B/18, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) and HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) of the folowing; Enemy submarine sighted close north of Amorgos Island at 0800 B/18. Further sighting suggested submarine patrolling line from Levitha to Scarpanto. (50)
21 Oct 1941 In the early evening, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) performs a special operation off Stavros (Thessaloniki region in Northern Greece) during which two Greek agents were landed.

The two men landed were SOE agent Ioannis Lekkas (alias KIP) and Greek Naval Officer Alexander (a pseudonym). Lekkas managed to contact Panagiotis Kanellopoulos, leader of the Omiros resistance group, who later fled to the Middle East and became Minister of Defence in the Tsouderos government in exile.
1630 B/21 - Arrived off Stavros. Selected landing place for the special operation on a beach to the north of Stavros.
1824 B/21 - Bottomed.
1919 B/21 - Surfaced and withdrew to seaward before commencing the special operation as the night was very clear.
1954 B/21 - Launched the canoe.
2010 B/21 - The canoe returned having successfully landed the agents. HMS Triumph withdrew to seaward at 12 knots for half an hour before starting a battery charge. (42)
23 Oct 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sank the Greek sailing vessels PI 241 Ioannis and SYR 457 / Agia Paraskeva with gunfire in the Gulf of Petali, Greece.
In the evening also the seconds special operation was carried out. [No further details available.]

0738 B/23 - Sighted a 50 tons caique to the southwards working along the coast under sail and motor. I had been Cdr. Woods intention not to attack small craft until later in the patrol but as this caique was flying the Nazi flag he reversed his decision.

0815 B/23 - Surfaced and opened fire with the 4" gun and Lewis gun. After 13 rounds of the 4" gun the caique sank in position 37°53'N, 24°05'E. The crew, Greeks, had meanwhile abandoned ship. Meanwhile a second caique, flying the Greek flag, was seen approaching from the northward. Triumph closed at full speed on the main engines.
0834 B/23 - Opened fire until the Greek crew began to abandon ship. Then closed and rammed the caique.

0903 B/23 - Dived and proceeded eastwards. Another small caique was seen approaching from the east but it was not attacked as HMS Triumph wanted to clear the area because the first two caiques had been sunk in view of an enemy wireless station and it was to be expected that A/S measures would follow.

1110 B/23 - Sighted a large (300 ton) three-masted schooner to the northward proceeding southwards along the coast. As it was not possible to close submerged no action was taken as Cdr. Woods did not want to run on the surface. Eight minutes later an aircraft was seen approached so this looked like the correct decision.
1330 B/23 - Sighted a two-masted schooner in the Mandri Channel. Several small caiques were seen inshore during the afternoon. Seven distant explosions were heard between 1430 B/23 and 1500 B/23.
1728 B/23 - Bottomed half a mile from Kaki Thalassa.
2024 B/23 - Surfaced and entered the cove.
2100 B/23 - The canoe left HMS Triumph.
2138 B/23 - The canoe returned following the submarine withdrew to seaward. Later course was altered to pass through the Thermia Channel (between Kea and Kithnos Islands). (42)
24 Oct 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) fired three torpedoes against the small Spanish merchant vessel Isora (316 GRT, built 1920) in the Gulf of Athens. No hits were obtained.
Isora had departed Piraeus at 0830 B/24.
0452 B/24 - In position 090° - Agios Georgios Island - 6 miles, sighted and evaded a trawler steering 140°.
0500 B/24 - Dived and commenced to patrol towards Phleva Island.
0833 B/24 - Sighted a northbound caique.
0840 B/24 - Sighted an eastbound caique.
1015 B/24 - Sighted two small fishing boats.
1016 B/24 - Sighted one trawler and one armed tug apparently minesweeping on course 140°.
1122 B/24 - Sighted a ship between Phleva Island and the mainland. Started attack.

1140 B/24 - Near position 37°42'N, 23°52'E fired three torpedoes from 700 yards. No hits were obtained and no explosions were heard afterwards (the shore was 7000 yards away). The target was now seen to be the small Spanish merchant vessel Isora. Shortly afterwards a patrol vessel appeared and also several aircraft were patrolling overhead so a gun action was out of the question.
1200 B/24 - Sighted a motor torpedo boat approaching from the direction of Gaidaro Island.
1304 B/24 - Sighted a large northbound schooner.
1535 B/24 - Sighted an armed trawler which looked like one of the South African whalers. This enemy vessel was northbound. This may have been the same vessel as had been seen at 0452 B/24.

The vessel sighted (also at 0452 B/24) may well have been the German auxiliary patrol vessel 12 V 4 which was the former British minesweeper HMS Widnes. She had left Pireaus for patrol at 0150 B/24 and returned there at 1800 B/24.
1930 B/24 - Surfaced in position 090° - Agios Georgios Island - 6 miles and patrolled to the south-west into the moon until moonset at 2110 B/24 then southwards until 2330 B/24. (42)

25 Oct 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Monrosa (6703 GRT, built 1920) in the Gulf of Athens, about 3 nautical miles north-west of Patroklou island, Greece in position 37°41'N, 23°53'E.
This was the 5th convoy transporting the Siena Division. They had sailed at 0950 B/25-1020 B/25 for Heraklion. At 1315 B/26, a German aircraft dropped three bombs and a minute later, Monrosa was hit.

She sank at 1321 B/25, two torpedoes hit the coast near Arsida. Sirio dropped twelve depth charges, an aircraft machine-gunned a spot on the water 300-400 metres farther north and Quintino Sella dropped nineteen depth charges, Libra another nine and the submarine chaser B 532 / Fedelsono (34 GRT, built 1939) five more in the same area.

At 1330 B/26, Sirio ordered Quintino Sella to go on with Sant'Agata and Libra to hunt the submarine while she proceeded to recover the survivors. Upon learning that Libra was short of fuel and must proceed to Athens, she ordered her to look for survivors while Sirio would hunt the submarine. In the meantime, Fanny Brunner (see below) was proceeding alone toward Arsida.

At 1403 B/26, in position 190° - Arsida - 3 miles, Sirio observed an aircraft dropping bombs on a submarine and MAS 534 was already dropping depth charges on the spot. Sirio joined and dropped another five depth charges.

At 1427 B/26, a German seaplane machine-gunned the sea in position 235° - Arsida - 2 miles, near Libra which was picking up the survivors. Quintino Sella had already dropped depth charges in the same area. This time Sirio dropped seven depth charges in three runs.

At 1440 B/26, Libra signalled that she had finished collecting the survivors and was proceeding to Piraeus. There were some 148 killed (one report mentions only 52 killed), 117 survivors, some of them picked up by the submarine chasers B 532 / Fedelsono (34 GRT, built 1939) and A.S. 40 / S. Bartolo (38 GRT, built 1933), the motor sailing vessel Marcomeni and MAS 534.

From 1505 B/26 to 1640 B/26, Sirio used a towing mine to criss-cross the area but without result, she then left the scene to catch up with Sant'Agata and Quintino Sella. MAS 538 arrived on the scene and was ordered by Sirio to hunt the submarine, the following day the MAS boat claimed it certainly sunk.
0507 B/25 - Dived four miles north of Agios Georgios Island and patrolled towards Phleva Island.
0650 B/25 - Sighted a motor caique 5 miles to the south of Cape Colonna proceeding on course 200°.
0655 B/25 - Sighted a small tug coast crawling eastwards having passed inside Gaidaro Island.
0710 B/25 - Sighted a two-masted schooner patrolling off Gaidaro Island until about 1200 B/25. No armament was seen on this schooner though.

0712 B/25 - Sighted an armed trawler (possibly the same one as seen at 1535 B/24), a motor torpedo boat and two small steam launches. The latter were apparently sweeping. All were steering a course of about 200° from Cape Colonna.
0803 B/25 - Sighted a motor drifter proceeding on course 140° from Phleva Island.

0825 B/25 - Sighted an Italian RD-class minesweeper and a tug. Both appeared to be sweeping on course 140° from Phleva Island. They were seen to return around 1100 B/25 having swept until Gaidaro Island. This may have been the Italian minesweeper RD 26 and the Italian auxiliary minesweeper R 187 which had been ordered to operate near Phleva Island.

0833 B/25 - Sighted a destroyer / torpedo boat which was subsequently identified as the Italian torpedo boat Monzambano proceeding on course 140° from Phleva Island. This torpedo boat remained on the course until she was out of sight.
This was indeed the Monzambano which had departed Piraeus at 0700 B/25 and arrived at Saloniki at 1025 B/26.

0855 B/25 - Sighted a minesweeper of the RD-class and one other vessel which looked like a Panigaglia-class minelayer. Both were apparently sweeping on course 140° from Phleva Island. These vessels too swept up to Gaidaro Island and then returned.
1120 B/25 - Sighted a motor torpedo boat steering 320° towards Phleva Island at high speed.

1235 B/25 - Sighted a hospital ship, believed to be the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) was seen passing Arsida Island at a range of 2 miles proceeding on course 140°. She remained on this course until out of sight. This was indeed the Gradisca which had departed Piraeus.

1245 B/25 - Sighted a convoy on the port quarter coming from the direction off Phreva Island. The composition of the convoy was two 6000-tons merchant ships escorted by one Sauro (or similar)-class destroyer, a Spica-class torpedo boat and one aircraft overhead. Started an attack on the leading ship. At the same time a convoy of one 4000-tons merchant vessel escorted by a torpedo boat (possibly Monzambano) and one aircraft were seen on the Port bow passing close to Gaidaro Island on an opposite course. All the merchant ships were flying light and thus not heavily laden.

The convoy with the 4000-tons merchant vessel was made up of the German transport Fanny Brunner (2464 GRT, built 1925). She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Libra. They had sailed from Portolago (Leros) at 1737 B/24 for Piraeus.Libra was detached to hunt the submarine which had sunk the Monrosa.
Cdr. Woods decided to attack the convoy of two ships firing four torpedoes at each of the merchant vessels.

1316 B/25 - Fired four torpedoes at the leading ship from 3500 yards. As Cdr. Woods put the periscope down he saw an aircraft diving straight towards [This was a German bomber escorting the convoy, it dropped three bombs.] He was therefore forced to abandon the attack on the second merchant vessel. Two heavy explosions occurred shortly afterwards, this caused to torpedo fire indicator light of torpedo tube No.5 to burn, so this tube was fired (in error).
1319 B/25 - While Triumph was going deep and taking evasive action three torpedo explosions were heard thought to be hits.
1320 B/25 - Heavily depth charging started that shook the submarine violently. Triumph retired westwards at slow speed.
1436 B/25 - Depth charging ceased, between 60 and 70 had been dropped. The area was patrolled during the whole afternoon by smaller A/S vessels and aircraft.

1530 B/25 - Returned to periscope depth. One ship making a lot of smoke, which was almost one of the southbound convoy, was entering the Phleva Channel, escorted by the 'Sauro-class' destroyer and one aircraft. There was no sign of the remaining ships. HMS Triumph then returned to 150 feet and retired to the southward.
1700 B/25 - A ship, sounding like a destroyer passed overhead when HMS Triumph was 6 miles to the west of Gaidaro Island.

1734 B/25 - A motor torpedo boat was patrolling at fairly high speed between Arsida and Gaidaro Islands. She dropped five depth charges between 1735 B/25 and 1805 B/25. This vessel was last heard at 1815 B/25. This may well have been the Italian motor torpedo boat MAS 538 which had been sent out to patrol near Arsida following the sinking of the Monrosa.
At the same time the smoke of what was probably a trawler was seen on the horizon between Agios Georgios and Gaidaro Islands, moving towards the latter. (51)
26 Oct 1941 At 0320 B/26, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived in position 160° - Syros - 10 miles.
At 0648 B/26, two tugs were seen close to the north of Paros. They were proceeding to the south-west. HMS Triumph closed but the tugs were soon lost from sight.

At 0755 B/26, HMS Triumph altered course to the northward. Subsequently Syros, Tinos and Mikonos harbours were inspected but found empty. A motor torpedo boat was seen entering Syros at 1215 B/26 from the northward.

At 2040 B/26, HMS Triumph surfaced in position 090° - Livada Point (Tinos) - 5 miles. Then proceeded to the north-east until moonset at 2315 B/26 after which course and speed were adjusted to be off Naxos at dawn/27. (42)
27 Oct 1941 At 0515 B/27, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) dived in position 300° - Cape Stavro (Naxos) - 8 miles. Set course to close Naxia.

0900 B/27 - A small coaster, a tug and six caiques were seen inside Naxia harbour. HMS Triumph then retired to the northward and inspected the harbours of Naoussa (Paros) and Parikia (Paros). Both were seen to be empty.
1915 B/27 - Surfaced 7 miles to the north of Paros and commenced to patrol to the north-east en on to the moon in the hope of intercepting any of the ships seen earlier in Naxia.
At 2045 B/27 - HMS Triumph turned to the south-west to pass through the Sifnou Channel and to arrive off Agios Georgios Island at dawn/28. (42)
28 Oct 1941 HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) was depth charged by the Italian torpedo boat Sirio. No damage is caused.

Sirio was escorting the Italian tanker Rondine (6468 GRT, built 1924) and the Italian transport Tagliamento (5448 GRT, built 1922) from Suda Bay to Piraeus. The Italian destroyer Quintino Sella was also part of the escort.

The convoy sailed from Suda at 1130 B/27. At 0010 B/28, Sirio sighted the submarine and immediately increased speed to ram, but the submarine rapidly dived, and the torpedo boat dropped eleven depth charges at 0022 B (not nine as reported by HMS Triumph). At 0034 B, she ordered Quintino Sella to proceed with the convoy while she lingered in the area to hunt the submarine. Nothing was observed and, at 0115 B, she left the area to rejoin the convoy, which arrived at Piraeus at 1040 B/28-1200 B/28.
0010 B/28 - In position 37°07'N, 24°49'E sighted what was thought to be two large caiques under sail, three nautical miles on the port beam. Altered course towards and brought up the gun crews.

0020 B/28 - When the range had decreased to 1500 yards it was seen that the targets were two merchant ships on an opposite course. All torpedo tubes were now brought to the ready, the gun crews were sent below and Triumph altered course to open up the range for a torpedo attack. The merchant ships were thought to be a 3000-tons tanker and a 4000-tons merchant ship. Enemy course was 340° at 9 knots.

0023 B/28 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat 1500 yards on the port bow. Dived to 80 feet but continued to turn to get on a firing course but HE of the merchant ships was soon lost due to the strong HE of the torpedo boat. An attack was therefore no longer possible. Triumph retired to the Westward at slow speed. Shortly afterwards the torpedo boat started dropping depth charges, it therefore seemed likely that Triumph had been spotted while still on the surface. In all nine depth charges were dropped but none were close. [The German KTB Aegean gives the position of the depth charging as 37°10'N, 24°45'E.]
0219 B/28 - Surfaced but almost immediately a motor torpedo boat was sighted lying stopped about 400 yards on the starboard beam. Position was about 4 miles north of Serifos Island.
0222 B/28 - Dived, apparently without being seen. Continued the passage through the Sifnou Channel.
0350 B/28 - Surfaced and continued westwards at 12 knots.
0525 B/28 - Dived 7 miles to the south-west of Cyclops Head (Serifos) and patrolled in the direction of Agios Georgios Island.
1242 B/28 - Heard HE, possibly from a motor torpedo boat, passing down the port side. Nothing was seen though.
1720 B/28 - Turned southwards.
2026 B/28 - Surfaced and patrolled to the southward until 0100 B/29.

2338 B/28 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2215 B/27 which contained the following orders; Leave your area P.M./30 and proceed to patrol during daylight/31 within 25 miles of Cape Sapthim, Elaphonisos Channel. Then proceed through the following positions; 'A' (36°08'N, 22°40'E), 'B' (34°32'N, 23°40'E), 'C' (32°46'N, 28°40'E) and then direct to Alexandria. Adjust speed to pass through positions 'B' and 'C' at approximately 1500 B/1 and 1200 B/3 respectively and arriving in the Alexandria Sanctuary at 0530 B/4. When your area you are to remain west of 23°50'E until south of Kithera. Intelligence of Vatika Bay area will be signalled later. (51)

29 Oct 1941 At 0646 B/29, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) sighted an armed trawler, believed to be the same one as had been seen on the 24th and 25th, and one small armed coaster patrolling between Agios Georgios Island and Cape Colonna. These hunted HMS Triumph for an hour during which one depth charge was dropped.

This may have been the German auxiliary patrol vessels 12 V 4 and 11 V 1 which had left Piraeus at 0245 B/29 to escort the Turkish merchant vessel Kurtulus (1756 GRT, built 1883) in. These two patrol vessels returned to Piraeus at 1915 B/29. The Turkish vessel had not arrived at the rendezvous.
At 0924 B/29, two motor drifters were sighted minesweeping on course 140° from Phleva Island. These were seen to turn back on reaching Gaidaro Island.
At 1120 B/29, a 150 ton petrol or water carrier was seen proceeding between Arsida and Gaidaro Islands on course 140°. This was possibly a Bulgarian vessel.
At 1215 B/29, a motor torpedo boat was seen to be patrolling 2 miles to the south-west of Arsida Island. It looked like this vessel suspected the presence of a submarine.
At 1518 B/29, smoke was sighted, probably from a trawler, patrolling off Cape Colonna.
During the day, numerous small caiques were seen close inshore.
At 1952 B/29, HMS Triumph surfaced in position 135° - Agios Georgios Island - 10 miles.

At 2230 B/29, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1601 B/29 was received but it was very corrupt. The signal also referred to another signal (timed 1303 B/29) which had not been received but was eventually received at 0122 B/30.
Course was then altered to Cape Malea and speed was increased.
At 2321 B/29, very heavy rain reduced visibility to less than 1000 yards. HMS Triumph altered course to pass between Falconera and Anti-Milos to make sure of clearing Karavi Rock.

The signal timed 1303 B/29 from Capt. S.1 contained the following information; Intelligence Vatika Bay Area. Considerable Caique traffic reported from Grinisha to Eeapolis and Kithera Channel with motor boats or motor Caiques armed with machine guns. Escorting motor torpedo boats have been reported occasionally. Regular air patrols reported to be carried out at 0800 daily by captured Sunderland and Dornier seaplanes. Considerable other air activity has been seen. Kithera Channel and Elaphonisos Island and Scutari are said to have a small garrison or troop concentration.
[We have been unable to find the text of the signal timed 1601 B/29.] (52)

30 Oct 1941 At 0122 B/30, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1303 B/29 [See the event for 29 October 1941 for the text of this signal]. Course was altered to proceeded to the Gulf of Nauplia.
At 0515 B/30, HMS Triumph dived in position 260° - Spetses - 3 miles and patrolled towards the head of the Gulf of Nauplia keeping to the eastward.
At 1100 B/30, HMS Triumph was 4 miles to the south of Nauplia. She then turned south down the western side of the Gulf.
During the day only three small fishing boat and one aircraft were sighted.
At 1942 B/30, HMS Triumph surfaced in position 180° - Spetses - 8 miles. She then continued southwards keeping 5 to 7 miles from the coast. (42)
31 Oct 1941 At 0506 B/31, HMS Triumph dived in position 220° - Cape Malea - 7 miles and commenced to close Vatika (Neapolis) Bay.

At 0730 B/31, HMS Triumph was off Neapolis but there was no sign of the Sunderland aircraft that had been reported there. Also no caiques were seen. HMS Triumph then retired to the south and then westwards through the Elaphonisos Channel.
At 1100 B/31, HMS Triumph patrolled north-westwards towards Githion turning west to proceed towards Skoutari at 1500 B/31.
At 1748 B/31, HMS Triumph turned to the south surfacing at 1932 B/31. She then left patrol to commence the return passage to Alexandria.
During the day only two small fishing boats had been sighted. (42)

1 Nov 1941 At 2200 B/1, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1531 B/1 which contained the following; Position, course and speed of enemy submarine sighted at 1220 B/1, 33°44'N, 24°48'E, 000°, 4 knots. This submarine was near missed by one bomb.
This was 38 miles bearing 141° from HMS Triumph's present position. An extra lookout was posted on the bridge and the zigzag was increased.

This referred to an aircraft attack by aircraft (Bristol Blenheim) 'Y' of 203 Squadron RAF on U-75 which had sustained some minor damage which was quickly repaired. The bomber was piloted by Pilot Officer Van der Water and dropped four general-purpose bombs. (53)
4 Nov 1941 Around 1215 B/4, HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) ended her 19th war patrol at Alexandria. (42)
20 Nov 1941 Around 1015 B/20, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) departed from Alexandria for her 20th war patrol. She is to patrol in the Aegean and to perform a special operation.
Patrol area in the Aegean between 37°00'N / 39°30'N and west of 25°40'E.
On departure exercises were carried out with HMS Thorgrim (Lt. J.B. Sparkes, RNR) and HMS Kingston Cyanite (Skr. F.A. Yeomans, RNR). (53)
20 Nov 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Triumph during her 20th war patrol see the map below.

21 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1623 B/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following info to HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN); Two enemy submarines sighted in position 35°55'N, 26°42'E at 1150 B/21 steering 180° diving and continuing submerged towards Kaso Straits.

The British were aware through ULTRA [DEFE3/834/pp. 772-773, 726, 725] of the departure of the Italian submarines Dagabur and Zaffiro from Leros. If sighted by an aircraft: 1) The position must have been at least 20 miles to the north, as the submarines could not have travelled that fast. 2) Probably only one submarine was sighted by aircraft (as Zaffiro had already turned back) but since they were aware that two submarines were sailing, they may have deduced that the other was in the vicinity (ULTRA later informed the British that Zaffiro had turned back).

In a signal timed 2332 B/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph; My 1623 B/21. Do not carry out special operation until further instructions. Establish A/S patrol in the vicinity of Cape Plaka (35°12'N, 26°20'E). (54)

22 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1545 B/22, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information to HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN); By 2332 B/21. One enemy submarine sighted in position 35°42'N, 26°40'E at 1205 B/22 proceeding on course 200°. Dived on sighting and continued on previous course.
As Zaffiro had returned to Leros at 0952 B/21, she turned back after only travelling about 15 miles. After repairs Zaffiro sailed again at 0650 B/22.
The submarine reported was the Dagabur. Her report only mentions her as diving at 0610 B/22 and surfacing at 1805 B/22 (no mention of diving on the 21st).

In a signal timed 1951 B/22, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph; My 1343 B/22. If nothing sighted by dawn/23, carry out your special operation and proceed to execute the previous orders. (54)
23 Nov 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) landed agents (including Chris M. Woodhouse) at Caruba Bay on the north coast of Crete (west of Candia / Iraklion).
In the afternoon reconnoitred Caruba and Grandes bays. Decided on Caruba Bay for the special operation as it afforded more shelter and less chance of interference.
2014 B/23 - Proceeded to a point 700 yards from the shore.
2019 B/23 - Both canoes were away.
2056 B/23 - The canoes returned. The operation had been carried out without difficulty or interference.
2105 B/23 - Proceeded to the eastward towards Candia keeping about 8 miles from the coast in case HMS Triumph had been sighted during the special operation. (42)

24 Nov 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian tug/salvage vessel Hercules (632 GRT, built 1910) and the tug Irini Vernikos inside Candia (Iraklion) harbour, Crete. The explosion of the torpedo caused also some damage to the German merchant vessels Norburg (2392 GRT, built 1922), that was being salvaged at the time, and to the Arkadia (1756 GRT, built 1927) behind her.
HMS Triumph also bombards the airport at Heraklion.
1141 B/24 - Closed Candia harbour to 3500 yards from the breakwater. In the harbour were 2 merchant ships of 3000 and 1500 tons secured to the mole.

1144 B/24 - Fired one torpedo against the 3000 tons merchant vessel But the torpedo was observed to hit a small vessel, thought to be an armed trawler, that was alongside the 1500 tons merchant vessel that was moored just astern of the 3000 tons vessel. The 'trawler' was seen to settle by the stern immediately.
A second torpedo was now fired against the 3000 tons merchant ship but it ran to the left and exploded at the far end of the harbour.
Triumph now left the area as air patrol were expected to arrive soon now.
1803 B/24 - Surfaced and set course towards the airport.
1822 B/24 - Opened fire with the 4" gun from 3500 yards. Shells were seen to explode along the line of the aerodrome and the gun emplacements. It was not possible to note if any damage was caused.
1825 B/24 - Ceased fire after 14 rounds. Cleared the area at high speed on the surface to the north-east.

1852 B/24 - Two or three guns of about 4" calibre opened fire from the direction of the harbour, firing salvos, two of which were observed to fall fairly close astern. After a further 10 minutes a bigger battery of two 6" guns opened up. No fall of shot was observed. During the next hour was very busy searching for the submarine with aircraft and searchlights but she was not detected.
2100 B/24 - Altered course to the north-westward. (42)
26 Nov 1941 At 0524 B/26, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) dived in position 270° - Agios Georgios lighthouse - 2.5 miles.
Between 0636 B/26 and 0721 B/26, nine depth charges were dropped at irregular intervals. These were not very close.

At 0647 B/26, faint HE was heard to starboard. It was too dark to see through the periscope. Course was altered to the southward to avoid closing a vessel which was thought to be dropping depth charges which could not be seen due to the darkness.
At 0720 B/26, course was altered to 320°.
At 0930 B/26, two caiques were seen inshore to starboard.
At 1155 B/26, a trawler was seen to pass between Arsida Island and the mainland on course 110°.
At 1230 B/26, the trawler seen at 1155 B/26 was now seen to come towards HMS Triumph. Course was altered to the westward and went deep.
At 1235 B/26, course was altered to the southward.
At 1300 B/26, a small steamer was sighted bearing 090° proceeding close inshore on course 320°.
At 1345 B/26, course was altered to 080°.
At 1500 B/26, course was altered to 160°. Speed was 2.5 knots.
At 1833 B/26, HMS Triumph surfaced near Agios Georgios Island. (42)
27 Nov 1941 At 0540 B/27, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) dived with Agios Georgios lighthouse, bearing 000°, range 5 miles.
At 0710 B/27, course was altered to the northward.
At 0810 B/27, course was altered to the north-westward.
At 1300 B/27, Arsida Island was bearing 073°, 2.8 miles. Visibility was good. Course was altered to 110°.
At 1510 B/27, the left hand edge of Gaidaro Island was bearing 005°, range 2.5 miles.

At 1655 B/27, HMS Triumph surfaced with the Angarlestro Point lighthouse bearing 066°, 2.5 miles. Set course for the Zea Channel en route to the vicinity of Volo. Lt. Huddart decided not to go through the Doro Channel as it was a very light night and it was to be expected that the submarine would be spotted.

At 2137 B/27, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0021 B/27 was received so course was set for the Thermia Channel to proceed to the newly ordered patrol area. [For the text of the signal see the other event for 27 November 1941.]

At 2219 B/27, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1845 B/27 was received which contained the following orders; My 0021 B/27. Leave area 'S' forthwith and establish patrol in area 'Q' within 50 miles of Pylos Light, Navarino. HMS Perseus (Lt.Cdr. E.C.F. Nicolay, DSO, RN) will be diverted. (Capt. S.l., 181+5B/27 to TRIUMPH). (42)

27 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 0021 B/27, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) and HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN); Cancel present patrol. Establish new patrol forthwith in the south-west Aegean in area between 36°50'N, 25°00'E, north coast Crete and line drawn 340°/160° through Antikithera light. Triumph in area 'S' and Thunderbolt in area 'T'. north-west and south-east respectively of line 045° from Antikithera light. (54)

28 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1145 B/28, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information to HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN); Mines reported between Pylos and Sphacteria Island. ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) reports encountered net when attempting to enter Navarino Bay. Aerial photographs revealed nothing. From outside Sokol attacked [on 21 November] one destroyer at anchor and obtained two probable hits.

In signals timed 1925 B/28 and 2051 B/28 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Triumph; Indicator net searched 30 feet stretched 130° from Pylos Light to mainland. H/F A/S transmissions occurred during the night across entrance. I) Net stretches from Pylos light in direction 130° Southern shore. It held Polish submarine Sokol proceeding at 2 knots at 40 feet and fired indicator charge. Sokol withdrew after few minutes by going astern, flooding and pumping proved ineffective, depth remaining constant. II) Net is invisible from surface and allows surface traffic to proceed unaffected. Calculated to stretch from 30 feet downwards and is patrolled by two motor boats who operate when indicator charge fires. Torpedoes fired successfully over net at destroyers in position 7 cables 010° from Pylos Island Light. III) A/S transmissions at frequencies above 10000 Kcs. recurring from sunset to sunrise across entrance. Shore defences observed near Pylos Island light and new earth works and emplacement noticed between bearings 116° and 135° from same position. Lamp communications between these two places and Pylos utilized frequently at night. (54)
29 Nov 1941 At 0514 B/29, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0133 B/29. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 29 November 1941.]

At 0543 B/29, HMS Triumph dived with Pylos Island Light bearing 110°, range 7.2 miles. Visibility was variable due to rain squalls. She then patrolled off the entrance to Navarinon Bay. No ships were seen inside the bay.
At 1846 B/29, course was set for the new patrol position as ordered in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0133 B/29.
At 2157 B/29, the Naval Officer in Command Malta's signal timed 2130 B/29 was received. [For more on this signal see the other event for 29 November 1941.] (42)

29 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 0133 B/29, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the following submarines, HMS Perseus (Lt.Cdr. E.C.F. Nicolay, DSO, RN), HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN), HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN); Operation BS. Establish a patrol line by 0400 B/30, 4 - 35°50'N, 19°35'E - 060° - 15 - 350° - 15. Submarines are to take up positions as follows; A) Perseus 35°50'N, 19°35'E. B) Thunderbolt. C) Trusty. D) Triumph.
In a signal timed 2130 B/30, the Naval Officer in Command Malta, ordered the patrol line to be established by 0100 B/29 and to be moved 20 miles - 240°.
In a signal timed 0150 B/30, the Naval Officer in Command Malta, ordered the patrol line to be moved 20 miles 330°.

This patrol line was deployed to intercept the Duilio-squadron (battleship Duilio, screened by the destroyers Granatiere, Alpino, Fuciliere and Bersagliere) which sailed from Taranto at 1730 B/29-1937 B/29 and was to cover the passage of three convoys for Benghazi [DEFE3/834/pp. 358, 367, 370, 373, 411-412, 413-413A, 418-419, etc.]:
1) Transport Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6310 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the Italian destroyer Giovanni da Verazzano sailed from Taranto at 1056 B/29. They arrived at Benghazi at 0700 B/1 December.

2) Transports Iseo (Italian, 2366 GRT, built 1918) and Capo Faro (Italian, 3476 GRT, built 1905), escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Procione, sailed from Brindisi at 1430 B/28. At 1045 B/30, Capo Faro was bombed and sunk by enemy aircraft and Iseo was ordered to proceed to Argostoli, while Procione picked up the survivors and reached Argostoli at 2330 B/30.

3) The Italian armed merchant cruiser Adriatico (1976 GRT, built 1931) unescorted, sailed from Argostoli at 2300 B/29. She was sunk by HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, RN) of Force K at 0300 B/1, 56 miles north of Benghazi.

In addition, the Italian light cruiser Luigi Cadorna was expected to sail at 2000 B/1 December at 18 knots for a supply mission to Benghazi [DEFE3/834/pp. 365, 366-366A]. However, she only sailed at 2320 B/7 December and, after a stop at Argostoli (from 0139 B/9 to 1626 B/10), finally reached Benghazi at 1115 B/11.
These were to pass through the area between 34°00'N and 37°00'N, and 18°00'E and 20°00'E on the 30th. The Duilio-group was to cover them on the same longitudes, but keeping north of 36°00'N.

The 7th Cruiser Division, made up of the light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta, Eugenio Montecuccoli and Muzio Attendolo, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere and Camicia Nera, sailed at 1300 B/30 from Taranto, they would also operate in the same area. Later, they were informed that the Duilio-group would be cruising from 0900 B/30 to 1900 B/30 between 36°40' N and 35°?? N (illegible on page) and 19°00'E and 19°40'E. It was then learned that two British cruisers and two destroyers had been sighted 100 miles north of Benghazi, so the light cruiser Giuseppe Garibaldi with the destroyers Corazziere and Carabiniere sailed from Messina at 2120 B/28-2220 B/29 to join the Duilio-group at 0900 B/30. After the two forces met, Giuseppe Garibaldi developed engine defects and at 1630 B/30, Duilio signalled the situation to Supermarina and that they were turning back before they could meet the 7th Cruiser division. The Duilio-group and Giuseppe Garibaldi arrived at Taranto at 1111 B/1-1447 B/1. The destroyers Corazziere and Carabiniere returned to Messina, respectively at 2349 B/1 and 0235 B/2. (54)
30 Nov 1941 At 0035 B/30, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) arrived in the originally ordered patrol position [as per the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0133 B/29].
At 0100 B/30, course was altered to take up the new patrol position as had been ordered in the Naval Officer in Command Malta's signal timed 2130 B/29.
At 0329 B/30, the Naval Officer in Command Malta's signal timed 0150 B/30 was received which ordered the patrol line to be moved 20 miles 330°.
At 0554 B/30, HMS Triumph arrived in the newly ordered patrol position. She then commenced to patrol within three miles from this position.
At 0643 B/30, HMS Triumph dived.
At 1001 B/30, 1330 B/30 and 1617 B/30, single explosions were heard.
Between 1248 B/30 and 1255 B/30, 35 distant explosions were heard.
At 1844 B/30, HMS Triumph surfaced. Visibility was good during the night, except for some occasional rain squalls. (42)
1 Dec 1941 At 0640 B/1, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) dived. Visibility was now moderate.
In the afternoon there were heavy rain squalls. Periscope visibility was only about a mile.
At 1720 B/1, the Naval Officer in Command Malta's signal timed 1121 B/1 was received with ordered her to patrol in area 'Q' off Navarinon.
At 1845 B/1, HMS Triumph surfaced. Visibility was good during the night. (42)

2 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1905 B/2, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) and HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN); Thunderbolt is to leave patrol P.M./4 and return to Alexandria through the following positions 36°24'N, 20°28'E, 34°32'N, 22°54'E, 33°44'N, 26°18'E, adjusting speed to pass through the second position about 1800 B/5 and arriving in the Alexandria Sanctuary at 0600 B/8. Triumph is to take over patrol in area 'Q 1' in addition to area 'Q' from 0001 B/5. (42)
2 Dec 1941 At 0238 B/2, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) fixed her position. The right-hand edge of Proti Island was bearing 013° and Sapienza Island lighthouse was bearing 076°.

At 0438 B/2, near Navarino Bay, a ship was sighted bearing 190° on an approximate opposite course. Range was about 5000 yards. It looked like a submarine until it started making smoke, when it was seen to be a small destroyer / torpedo boat with a single funnel very close abaft the bridge. Speed was increased to 14 knots and a retiring turn was made to make an attack. The enemy however proceeded too fast to get near enough to fire with any chance of success. It was thought speed of the enemy was about 20 knots on an approximate course of 320°. HMS Triumph then proceeded to the south-eastward.

The only torpedo boat that we could place in the general area is the Pegaso which sailed from Suda Bay at 2300 B/1 and arrived at Argostoli at 1835 B/2. She could not have reached Navarino at 0438 B/2 unless there is an error in the time in the report of HMS Triumph [There is no log to cross check the time.]
At 0620 B/2, HMS Triumph dived with Sapienza Island lighthouse bearing 004° at 14 miles.
At 1023 B/2, smoke was sighted bearing 110°. This was in sight for only a few minutes and then disappeared. No HE was heard.
At 1840 B/2, HMS Triumph surfaced with Sapienza Island lighthouse bearing 040° - 8.5 miles. HMS Triumph then withdrew to the southward for charging. (42)
6 Dec 1941 At 0622 B/6, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) dived and proceeded inshore towards Navarino Bay.
At 0834 B/6, Sapienza Island lighthouse was bearing 092° - 4 miles.

At 0932 B/6, a two-masted schooner was sighted leaving Navarino Bay. She was flying Greek flags from each masthead. She remained in the approaches to the bay until 1230 B/5 when she returned to harbour.
At 1055 B/6, no shipping was seen in Navarino Bay.
At 1832 B/6, HMS Triumph surfaced.
At 2337 B/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1715 B/6 was received. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 6 December.] (42)

6 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1715 B/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) and HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN); Leave patrol P.M./7 and return to Alexandria as follows; Triumph through positions 36°10'N, 21°26'E, 34°28'N, 23°16'E and 33°22'N, 27°54'E, thence direct adjusting speed to pass the second position about 1900 B/8 and arrive in the Alexandria Sanctuary at 0530 B/11. Thrasher through positions 38°00'N, 18°50'E, 35°00'N, 21°20'E and 34°24'N, 25°40'E thence direct adjusting speed to pass the second position at approximately 2300 B/9 and arrive in the Alexandria sanctuary at 0530 B/15. (55)

8 Dec 1941 At 0440 B/8, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) passed her signal timed 2320 B/7 to the Capt. S.1. This signal contained the following; No enemy ship in or around Navarino during the last six days. Flight of six unescorted transport or bomber aircraft fly over Navarino low about 1030 B most days course north or south. [a weather report was also included in the signal]. (56)
11 Dec 1941 Around 1115 B/11, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) ended her 20th war patrol at Alexandria. (42)
19 Dec 1941 In the evening of the 18th, the Italian submarine Scirè launched three SLC (Siluro Lenta Corsa = Slow moving torpedo also known as Maiale = pig) near Ras El Tin.

These managed to pass through the gate with ships entering harbour. The boom had been opened several times; Around 2359 B/18 the boom was ordered to be closed after the sloop HMS Flamingo (Cdr. R.J.O. Otway-Ruthven, RN) and the tugs HMS Roysterer and HMS St. Monance had just entered.

At 0024 B/19, the boom, which had not yet been completely closed was ordered to be opened again. It was open by 0040 B/19 after which first the light cuisers HMS Naiad (Capt. M.A.H. Kelsey, DSC, RN flying the flag of Rear-Admiral P.L. Vian, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN). Followed not long after by the destroyers HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, DSC, RN), HMS Maori (Cdr R.E. Courage, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Legion (Cdr. R.F. Jessel, RN). The destroyer HrMs Isaac Sweers (Cdr. J. Houtsmuller, RNN) had been with them but was a little behind and entered at 0120 B/19 after which the boom was ordered to be closed which was completed at 0150 B/19.

At 0225 B/19 the boom was ordered to be opened again which was completed at 0242 B/19 after which the destroyers HMS Jervis (Capt. P.J. Mack, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Kimberley (Cdr. J.S.M. Richardson, DSO, RN), HMS Kipling (Cdr. A. St.Clair Ford, RN) and HMAS Nizam (Lt.Cdr. M.J. Clark, RAN) entered and the boom was ordered to be closed at 0300 B/19. It was reported shut at 0315 B/19.
