One of the Maiale crew mentioned in interrogation that they had been able to get in together with three big destroyers, this might therefore have been HMS Sikh, HMS Maori and HMS Legion.

All three crews of the Maiale managed to place the explosive warheads. Warheads were attacked to the main targets, the battleships HMS Queen Elizabeth (Capt. C.B. Barry, DSO, RN, flying the flag of Admiral Sir A.B. Cunningham, GCB, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Valiant (Capt. C.E. Morgan, DSO, RN) and the tanker Sagona (Norwegian, 7554 GRT, built 1929).
At 0325 B/19, two Italians were picked up which were on HMS Valiant's mooring buoy. Interrogation produced no result.
At 0547 B/19, an explosion occured under the stern of the Sagona causing massive damage to the tanker. The bow of the destroyer HMS Jervis who was alongside was also damaged by the explosion.
At 0606 B/19, an explosion occured under the "A" turret of HMS Valiant. She was heavily flooded forward and was down by the bow.

At 0610 B/19, HMS Queen Elizabeth was rocketed by an explosion underneath the boiler rooms and as a result three boiler rooms were flooded. She was unable to raise steam and the submarines HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) and HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) were brought alongside either side to provide electricity. HMS Trusty leaves after a few hours but HMS Triumph remained alongside until 22 December.
Neither battleship could be docked immediately due to their current draught.
All six crew of the Maiale were captured by the British.
26 Dec 1941 Around 1600 B/26, HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) departed Alexandria for her 21th war patrol. She is to patrol in the Aegean and to perform special operation ISINGLASS.
En-route to her patrol area she was to pass through position 33°06'N, 27°12'E.

After performing the first part of the special operation she had freedom to patrol in the Aegean between 36°00'N / 39°00'E and 23°00'E / 25°30'E. It is to be expected that Lt. Huddart would have elected to spent most of his time to the south of Piraeus / Gulf of Athens as most of the enemy traffic in the patrol area either originated there or went there. (57)

30 Dec 1941 HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) reported by signal a successful first part of the special operation. Captain G. Atkinson, Lt. J.W.C. Craig (both from MI9) and wireless operator Diamantís Arvanitopoulos (code name DIAMOND) are landed at Antiparos. Triumph was supposed to pick them up with a party of stragglers who are being rounded up again on 9 January 1942 but she failed to show up at the rendezvous. The special operation will turn sour when the whole party will be captured by the Italians. Atkinson who has also offered his services to SOE (without disclosing it to MI9) will be found with a list of the principal personalities of the Greek Resistance on him. A list he should have memorized and destroyed. This will enable the Italian police to arrest several of them. In 1943, Atkinson, who has murdered an Italian officer, will be executed with Arvanitopoulos and Greeks who have sheltered them.

The signal from HMS Triumph was timed 2121 B/30 and contained the following; Part one of the special operation completed. Following information received. 20000 Paratroops Messaria Plain, Crete. 300 Light Tanks, Light Mechanical Transport and large quantity aircraft torpedoes Heraklion. Source considered reliable. [A weather report was also included.] (58)

4 Jan 1942 At 1450 B/4, in position 36°07'N, 24°14'E, the motor cutter Sofia [later renamed Netztender 36] from Netzsperrgruppe Süd reported being missed by a torpedo. A bubbletrack had been sighted following which depth charges were dropped. This could have been HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) but the attack could also well be bogus. (59)

5 Jan 1942 At 1330 B/5, a submarine was reported south-east of Milos proceeding on course 250° proceeding at 4 knots. Was this HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) ?. If this sighting was genuine, than this is quite possible as not other Allied submarine was in the area. (60)

9 Jan 1942 At 1145 B/9, the tug Taxiarchis towing the large lighter Rhea reported being missed by a torpedo near Cape Sounion. The torpedo exploded on hitting the shore so this reported attack may well be genuine. If so the attacker must have been HMS Triumph (Lt. J.S. Huddart, RN) which then must have been patrolling inside the minefield (150 UMB mines, laid at a depth of 12 meters) off Cape Sounion which had been laid by the Italian minelayer Barletta on 4 December 1941 between positions 37°36.1'N, 23°58.7'E to 37°36.3'N, 24°03.3'E. If so it seems likely the row of mines had been detected by running the MDU (Mine Detector Unit) with which HMS Triumph was equipped.
The tug and barge were eastbound and had left Piraeus that morning. They arrived at Lavrio at 1430 B/9.

During the search for the wreck of HMS Triumph three British Mk.VIII torpedoes have been found near Cape Sounion. These lay more or less parallel to each other so it seems likely these were fired at the same target but missed and sank to the bottom at the end of their running range. Were these three torpedoes fired by HMS Triumph?. We will never know for sure but this is very well possible.

If so, were these three torpedoes also fired at the tug and lighter? This seems doubtful, normally four torpedoes would not be fired at a tug towing a lighter, that would be overkill and the Commanding Officer would certainly be criticised by the Commanding Officer of the submarine flotilla upon his return from patrol for the torpedo expenditure on such a small target. On the other hand, this was only Lt. Huddart's second war patrol and his last for the moment in the eastern Mediterranean as HMS Triumph was to return to the U.K. for refit after this patrol. It seems likely he was very keen to put something in his bag before leaving patrol so he may have opted to fire multiple torpedoes to have a better chance of a hit and then face the music regarding the torpedo expenditure on this target on his return from patrol.

Another possibility is that if the three torpedoes found were indeed from HMS Triumph is that these had been fired at another target. The German submarine U-97 which rounded Cape Sounion on the surface a few hours before the Taxiarchis and Rhea then seems a likely candite as being the target for these three torpedoes.

U-97 arrived at Salamis from a patrol in the eastern Aegean at 1015 B/4 having been escorted in by the patrol vessel 12 V 4 which had made rendezvous with the submarine near Gaidaro Island at 0745 B/4. U-97 thus must have passed the area off Cape Sounion around 0730 B/4.

U-97's 0800 B/4 position was grid CK 8985 which is centred in position 37°39'N, 23°54'E. Sunrise at Athens was around 0742 B/9 so it is possible the submarine had been attacked around dawn. U-97 had not reported an attack though but it is possible the torpedo tracks were not sighted in the light conditions at the time of the possible attack on her. (61)
We recommend reading the following book One submarines secret war against the Axis (offsite link) by Gav Don for the full details on the authors account on the loss of HMS Triumph.
A brief narrative below.

HMS Triumph was due to pick up the escapees and the party in the early hours of 9 January 1942 or in the early hours of 10 January 1942. It is thus to be expected that HMS Triumph would be off Antiparos at midnight either on the night of 8/9 January or 9/10 January.

If the attack reported by the tug Taxiarchis is genuine, and given the explosion on the torpedo hitting the shore, this is likely this would have meant that HMS Triumph was off Cape Sounion shortly before noon on the 9th thus having missed the rendezvous for the first night. This could be explained due the fact that Lt. Huddart had made no sinkings yet on this patrol, which was to be HMS Triumph's last before returning to the U.K. to refit. Likely, he must have been very eager to put something 'in his bag' before leaving patrol and return to Alexandria. He may thus have elected to stay on patrol for an additional day and proceed to Antiparos on the second night.

To make it in time this would have meant that HMS Triumph would need to depart from the Cape Sounion area around noon on the 9th so this just fits as the attack on the Taxiarchis was around 1145 hours.

If the attack on the Taxiarchis was made from within the row of mines off Cape Sounion, which is likely, then HMS Triumph would need to clear it before turning to the south-east towards Antiparos. The minefield to the south of Cape Sounion was laid by the Italian minelayer Barletta on 6 December 1941 and contained 150 UMB mines. These were laid at a depth of 12 meters, from position 37°36.1'N, 23°58.7'E to 37°36.3'N, 24°03.3'E.

It seems likely and logical that Lt. Huddart would have elected to proceed westwards towards Gaidoro Island as this route would mean staying on the enemy shipping route to Crete as long as possible and possibly being able to make another attack if something came along.
From a point to the west of the above row of mines, it appears a route had been plotted to pass to the east of Agios Georgios Island towards the Strait between the islands of Sifnos and Kimolos.

While taking that route, which would require the minimum of course changes and would also have the least interference of the currents, HMS Triumph ended up in the minefield which had been laid by the Barletta on 04 December 1941 and was made up of 108 EMC mines which had been laid at a depth of 12 meters from position from 37°29'N, 23°57.3'E to 37°35.4'N, 24°00.3'E (Agios Georgios - Cape Sounion).

Most likely HMS Triumph thus hit one of these mines. However that damage to HMS Triumph is far greater then is to be expected from hitting a mine. The author of the book linked to above is convinced that when hitting the mine HMS Triumph was in the process of reloading the torpedo tubes and that an internal explosion had occurred as a result of the initial mine explosion. Such an internal explosion seem the only logical explanation for the amount of damage that is done to the front part of the submarine which is virtually all missing. (62)

7 Jun 2023 On this day it was announced that the Greek researcher / diver / ROV operator Kostas Thoctarides and his team had found the wreck of HMS Triumph in the Aegean. The wreck lays in a little over 200 meters of water and there is massive damage to the front part of the submarine which is apparently mostly missing.

The exact location of the wreck is not yet released to the public but the indicative location of the wreck seems to indicate that HMS Triumph most likely hit a mine in the minefield laid by the Italian minelayer Barletta on 4 December 1941 (see below).
For some video footage of the wreck see this video (offsite link).
Postwar it was suggested that HMS Triumph might have been lost on one of four minefields laid in the general area by the Italian minelayer Barletta in the weeks before the loss of HMS Triumph.
These four minefields had been laid as follows;
04 December 1941: 108 EMC mines, laid at a depth of 12 meters, from position from 37°29'N, 23°57.3'E to 37°35.4'N, 24°00.3'E (Agios Georgios - Cape Sunion).
06 December 1941: 150 UMB mines, laid at a depth of 12 meters, from position 37°36.1'N, 23°58.7'E to 37°36.3'N, 24°03.3'E (South of Cape Sunion).
23 December 1941: 108 EMC mines, laid at a depth of 12 meters, from position from 37°32'N, 23°39.2'E to 37°30.5'N, 23°47.5'E (Agios Georgios - Poros).
23 December 1941: 90 EMC mines, laid at a depth of 12 meters, from position from 37°30.2'N, 23°37.3'E to 37°29.3'N, 23°44.5'E (Agios Georgios - Poros).

Canadian researcher, Platon Alexiades, always considered the most likely cause of the loss of HMS Triumph was that she was mined to the south of the Gulf of Athens which he also mentions in his book Target Corinth Canal 1940-1944 (offsite link) on pages 76 and 77.
